

[Shawn] There is a chance that one of us is going to die on this call and we’re just going to have to push module five to next week. Hopefully that’s not the case. But some of you are pretty crafty with your questions, holy smokes. All right, let’s dive in. We’ll do the same format we did last time, I’ll read the question and then we’ll just bounce it back and forth. We’re going to try to limit ourselves, André’s going to punch me in the face if I talk long. What did you say? Like 10 minutes per question.

[André] 10 minutes, yeah.

[Shawn] That’s laughable ’cause that means we would still be on the call for three days. So we’re gonna try to get through these questions, obviously. And if there are questions that we’re answering 12 different ways, then we’re just going to call that out. If we’ve already answered the question and it’s just some minor variation, maybe we’ll address the minor variation.

But I have a feeling, a lot of these questions are the same question, just asked in different ways. But, what do I know. All right, here’s the first question. This is from Julie. Julie says, can you go over an example in a live workshop of where the two narrative themes interacted and how they could be transplanted. For example, if my overarching theme is natural movement and my soap opera series was lifestyle. How do they intersect? Or don’t they? Or do they, a little more or a lot? Hey, you should answer this one first, André. That sounds like fun.

[André] Yeah, let me try and understand this thing first. Two narrative themes.

[Shawn] Yeah, we had two themes. In the manifesto that we had a broader theme and then we had a sub-theme in the enrollment around pride. So yeah, they’re interacting intersecting.

[André] Yeah, I think so. We did a PLS promotion and, you know, a soap opera series, at least that’s what’s written in the question, is something that’s not necessarily, well, that’s not time bound per se, you know. It’s typically within your automation somewhere. So it’s happening at any point in time, whenever somebody responds to a link trigger, they move into or they go into a soap opera series.

So, unless within that soap opera series, you’re drawing people’s attention to something external outside of that soap opera series. So if you point them to a manifesto. But that’s a bit more difficult because then you’re asking them to go somewhere, read it, interact with it, come back. I think it’s just easier to think about having a single narrative theme across a soap opera series. At least, for my simple mind, I mean, you know, none of this stuff is set in stone and the whole idea of themes and controlling ideas.

It can feel a bit like trying to herd cats sometimes or like fog it’s a bit difficult to wrap your head around sometimes but at least for me, I just have a single idea of what a theme is gonna run across a certain soap opera series. And then I’ll make sure that the narrative, you know, uses that as the spine and then pulls people forward towards something. Yeah. What do you think about that?

[Shawn] This is a hard question. I want to make sure people don’t get too wound up about theme and narrative arc and other things because if you make it capital T theme, then it feels like there’s weight associated. To me the theme is just home base. What the hell is this thing about, right. Like from beginning to end, if there’s like a thing that runs through all of it, like what, what is that thing?

So Julie’s example, if the overall theme is natural movement you know, first of all, you need to make that clear to the audience. What do you mean by natural movement? As opposed to unnatural movement? Like, these are the kinds of questions that come for me, out of theme. And then I would want to connect that to this sub-idea of lifestyle, right. Like it’s probably not natural that we sit in chairs all day, right. So that’s a good way to connect larger theme to sub-theme. So, okay, Julia just posted, it’s how you move when you’re in nature, okay.

So sitting in an ergonomic chair is not how we move in nature, right. So, you know, par core and other things are, you know, sort of based on this idea of natural movement. So it would be, first of all, I need to understand, does my audience know what I’m talking about? And then, as I explained what they’re talking about, how can I connect that to things that they do understand, using metaphor or daily activities or if I’m contrasting natural movement with, sort of an unnatural movement, you know, like, they go in the gym and they do these fixed movements in the machines. And why that wouldn’t make a lot of sense. So it’s the natural movement in the lifestyle. Things are they’re woven together. Now which is the larger theme? And which is a sub-theme? I mean, to me it would seem like in this example, natural movement is the larger theme.

And it’s almost comparing and contrasting. Because natural movement is a beautiful example of an implication of an open loop, right. The moment you say natural movement, my first question is, well, what the hell is unnatural movement? There’s implication there. So that immediately creates tension. So you’re off to the races already with a good theme. And then it’s a matter of making sure you don’t reduce that tension too quickly. And where is it going? That’s also informs a lot of themes. If the theme is natural movement and you say everything you need to say in the first email about natural movement, then the theme isn’t natural movement. Because the theme has to carry the freight from beginning to end of the series.

So the way to think about this is, okay, how am I going to set this frame of natural versus unnatural movement or natural movement? I think it is natural versus unnatural, right? It’s, how do you move in nature versus, how do you move when you’re in the gym, in a machine. Or how do you move when you’re you’re using weights or whatever it is. Like, you’re comparing those two things. But if it’s connecting it to lifestyle, to me it’s just, this gets woven. These two things are actually, I’m imagining sort of two threads woven together.

Because you’re constantly going from high-level theme to connecting the dots for your audience. Lifestyle isn’t as much of a theme in here, as it’s almost a vehicle. And really, sometimes theme is just vehicle too. And theme’s the thing that we’re riding from beginning to end. It’s the thing that makes the whole thing stick together. If we can look at the whole thing, so you’re like, what held these nine emails together?

Oh, it’s this idea of natural movement versus unnatural movement. And they intersect it, all of these different points through metaphor, through example, through, you know, story, through a character. I mean, that’s, I don’t know if that’s, I feel like we’re not really answering the question well. Julie, feel free to drop more details into the question in Zoom and we’ll come back at this ’cause I’m not sure, we may have needed to ask additional questions.

[André] Yeah. I think I’ll just quickly add something that you mentioned when you started answering the question. And I just want to draw attention to it ’cause it’s important. Whenever I write something and share it with my writing coach and then she comes back and she says, you needed to find what that means.

And she’s always picking at me because, in my head, I know what I mean. And people won’t necessarily, you know, just because I think that most of our audience know what a pop means, you know, a pocket of people, well, that should be defined. So then someone reading it that maybe doesn’t quite know what that means, then everybody’s on the same hymn sheet and then they can enjoy the impact of the series. So, yeah. So just more broadly, we need to define what we mean, so it’s clear. Because when things aren’t clear, then it’s difficult for people’s attention to stay focused.

[Shawn] And that’s the curse of knowledge right there. And all of us deal with this. It’s not just Julie, in this example, All of us have the curse of knowledge. When you know something about a topic, it’s very easy to fill in the things that people who don’t know about the topic don’t know. We can fill in, assuming they know those things. And it’s, really have to take a step back and as if to say, you know, like, you know, how would I explain this to someone who does not know anything about this?

And when we use a term, anything, we have to take a step back and say, well, wait a minute, what does that mean? Because it creates incredible opportunities when we do that because there’s just so much more we can explore when we don’t automatically fill in the blanks. All right, these next questions are from Rich. It looks like there are like 45 questions in this one group of questions. I’m gonna do these in two parts, I think.

All right, so here’s the question. Where do you guys start? What’s your anchor to build from? I’m guessing it’ll usually be with the product because the product is the end point. But I’m trying to assume nothing. Do you look at the offer as a destination and build backwards, outwards or something else? Like you find one customer or desired outcome, facts, drive that stake into the ground and build out from there? We both leverage narratives so much, okay. So, alright. André, do you wanna tackle this first? Where do we start? I think we probably have different answers to this. Maybe, I don’t know. I’ll be curious to see if we have any coherent answers to this at all.

[André] I think, broadly speaking, maybe most people’s entry point in, will be a product. They’ll have something, you know, they would have codafide some expertise and put it into this thing, whether it’s free or paid. And that becomes the natural entry point. I think that’s where lots of people start. Even if they’re building an audience first, you know, they’ll be known for something. Or they’ll try and put themselves out there, around an idea. Yeah. Then I think, you know, building a personal brand, where the brand, this idea, just a way of thinking and seeing the world that’s maybe slightly more challenging because it’s not a specific thing.

It’s not a product. So, you know, for us at TLB, we have a more general entry point. But then we have all of the courses there that create framings, you know. So there’s all these different things that people come into our world thinking about. Or they’re there for a certain reason. So we send them through our more general thing. And then through that, through the context of that, we use, you know, automation to try and help connect them with points of interest and then take it from there. There’s no right or wrong answer. It’s just how you choose to do it. But I think it’s easier to start with the product. I think that’s my gut feeling anyway. I mean, that’s how I started, I think.

[Shawn] And it’s really, it comes down to a question if you get on an airplane, how do you get from point A to point B? Well, the first thing you need to know is where’s point B, right. If you get on a plane, like, hey, I’d like to go somewhere. Where? Right. And that’s the first question. I’ll think out loud for a second. Yeah, I think this is how I think about it. I say that because I just, you know, at this point it’s unconscious competence.

I just do it and then, who knows? So yes, you have to know where you’re going. From my perspective, the reason you need to know where you’re going is because then there’s no contrast without A and B. A is where they are. B is where you’re taking. Right. In order to have that contrast built in, that structural tension that Robert Fritz talks about so much, you have to have those two points. You have to have this desired future reality and then their current relationship to that future reality. And if there’s no distance between those things, then there’s no tension.

So yes, you have to start with that. And the way I think about it is, I start with the where. Where am I taking them? And what are the emotions associated with that destination? When they get there, how are they going to feel? And then, where are they now and how do they feel right now? And what’s the Delta between how they feel now and how they want to feel? In that space, in the difference between how they currently feel and how they would like to feel, is all of your playground, right. Because ultimately, you know, it’s just that old saying, we buy with emotion and we justify with logic, right. We are emotional creatures. We want to feel differently. We wouldn’t buy things if we didn’t want to feel differently.

At the end of the day, there’s nothing more true than that. To do this work well, I have to understand how somebody wants to feel, how I think they want to feel as a result of whatever it is I’m showing them. And it could just be information. It doesn’t have to be, I mean, it could be an SOS without a product at the end. It could just be an idea that I want somebody to internalize and that once they understand this idea, then they’re going to feel better as a result. And so it doesn’t always have to be a product. But there does have to be a destination because a destination sets up the tension and it tells us what the emotions are. And then this question here, where do we start?

What’s the anchor to build from? I mean, you build from A to B tension. Where they are, where they want to go, the emotional difference between those things. And then, you know, this is where it’s just magic. Like sometimes there’s a quote. Sometimes there’s an idea that pops into your head. Like when pride popped into our head, in the conversation for the AOE enrollment. Like something just shows up. And that may be different for me ’cause I need a starting point. I can’t just sit down and start writing and, you know, hope I wind up someplace.

I need to know where I’m starting. It could be a scene from a movie, right. One of the emails we wrote a year and a half or so ago, was about an experience I had when I was working with a client in Baltimore. And it was a scene from a movie, from the movie “Heat”. That thing was like, that was the anchor that everything came from, in that email because it was such a poignant experience. Yeah, I mean, I think I’ll leave it at that. Let’s move on to the next, you good with that? Anything to add before we move on to the next question?

[André] No, no, go for it.

[Shawn] Okay. Can we think of our customers as our main character? As the hero in our marketing, including the product as the narrative art they go through to change and grow, like the product is the third act. So the first act is outward facing to available everyone. Your ad is your opening scene. The hook the brick building tree, while simultaneously letting them know they’re in good hands.

Second act happens in email, after opt-in and pulling them forward through some uncomfortable but important, big realization. So the third act, which has only experienced through purchase of and going through your product. And that’s an interesting idea. Why don’t you jump on this? I’m just gonna do this, the whole thing. I’m just gonna have, like, give myself time to think about stuff.

[André] Yeah, I like the way that Rich has framed that. Yeah, I don’t have any issues with that. I don’t think. Do we see our customers as our heroes? As the main character, the hero? Yeah, I think partly what we do, you know, some of the people that we serve are people that serve others. So we serve creators that have their own audiences, so. Partially one of the things we do, is we try and make them look smart in front of their customers. By helping them see the world in a different way, you know. And you can go backwards as well.

At some point, you know, we’ve all had our own journeys and had to battle along the way. And through that journey, we’ve learned things. And, you know, if you wanna follow the hero’s journey, you know, at some point we become a guide to somebody else. And through that lens, we can then help them move forward. And I think, in that context, they are the hero. We’re helping them on their journey through our own expertise and the fact that we’ve been on their journey.

And yeah, we can give them tools, products, ideas, insights, that, you know, I’ve said this so many times, whereas those things can metaphorically be magical items. And it’s through those magical items that are not gonna get them necessarily directly to their destination, to point B. But it’s gonna help them get there. There’s always gonna be obstacles along the way. So, yeah, I feel like I’m rambling off the path now, so, bring us back.

[Shawn] I mean, this is straight out of Donald Miller’s story brand, you know, playbook, right. So the customer is the hero. The hero has an external emotion. That is indicative of a deep internal need. And then the hero meets the guide. The guide has empathy first, authority next. And the guide has a plan. And the plan is related to a future desired reality, which is contrasted with a future that they don’t want. Like, that’s story brand in a nutshell.

The challenge I’ve always had with Donald Miller’s framework, is that it doesn’t tell you how to create a campaign. It tells you how to think about your audience in relationship to a campaign. But then if you don’t sit down and think to yourself, like, okay, look, let me name our hero. And I’m going to tell the story about the hero who meets me and I’m the guy. It just gets really weird. So it’s, I think it’s a thinking tool, right.

So the first thing you need to imagine and again, this maps to what we talked about a second ago. There’s there’s contrast, right, the hero feels a certain way. Your customers feel a certain way right now, there’s an external manifestation of that, which could be like frustration. Like they’re trying to get something done and they don’t know how to do it. But that what really matters is the internal sense they have as a result. Maybe they feel like a failure because of this frustration. You can speak to the frustration and scratch the surface or you can speak to the sense of failure and really light the other person up. Because sometimes your audience hasn’t made that connection. Like they have a soup of feelings and they all get jammed together and they don’t feel good as a result, they want to feel better.

And the first thing we do is, we sort of deconstruct for them, that there’s, you know, what are those feelings? Why are they important? And be very clear that they’re valid, right. And we’ve all experienced this, we’ve all felt like, I assume, I know I certainly have, we’ve all felt like failures at one time or another. We’ve all felt frustrated at one time or another. But when we speak to these core, deep, visceral needs. Or at least when we recognize them, that’s the source of empathy. That’s how we connect with an audience and say, I get it.

I’ve been there. I felt what you feel. And I know what it feels like to feel better. And I can show you how. Like, that’s it. The rest, everything else, just details. If you can do those two things really well, if you can define for somebody a future where they feel different and explain that to them and you can compare that to where they are. And then you can honestly say that I can take you from point A to point B. Everything else just happens. Everything else are sort of the details. I’m gonna do a little commercial for the art of email here, right now.

Because I’ve been thinking about this. I wanted to start with this. All of you own it, right. Everybody gets it now. You own the course, just please do it. Like, please, please do it. Because this works, right. And yes, you know, the going through all the questions and this is all really important. But what’s more important than anything else, is ass in chair, fingers on keyboard, making something happen and putting it out in the world. And then you’ll get some feedback and you’ll make some changes. You have the thing. You own the thing that’s going to make all the difference in your life if you put it in motion. So please God, put it in motion. Do them. I’m trying not to swear. I’m gonna end up swearing today. So if any of you are offended, I apologise. All right, next question.

[André] Yeah, I just wanna say that, you know, at of email is not an intellectual exercise, it is a practical course. So we’ve tried our best to explain things in a way. But it’s easy to over intellectualize and try and get all these little things understood to the point.

You know, most of us have figured this out by doing the work. I mean, God knows, I figured this, I mean, I did this stuff because it just felt right. And at some point, when I had to look back at why, you know, try and, you know, externalize how come this thing is working so well, I had to look backwards for that and try and figure it out that way, as opposed to trying to figure it out in my head and then move forward. You know, that’s a recipe for inaction. So, yeah, that’s what I’ll say on that.

[Shawn] A word from our sponsors. We’re our own sponsors. The next questions is from Gustav. My industry is really competitive and user behaviors, like you sign up for a lot of different comparison sites, education sites, solar installers, to get a quote at the same time. Therefore I can’t wait for the relationship building series to be done. And so I nudged them to get a quote from my partners. That’s not true Gustav, come on. If the partners had gotten the request to the quote from another side, I won’t get paid.

Also, it’s in the best interest of the lead to get a quote as soon as possible, as they otherwise wont be able to properly understand their options when it comes to getting solar. So any ideas, comments, suggestions to sell them, to get a quote in the relationship building serious about hurting the relationship building aspect of the email series. I’m gonna tackle this one first. So Gustav, I have a 15 kilowatt or 13 kilowatt, $46000 solar array sitting in my backyard that was installed this summer. So let me tell you about my user behavior.

I do exactly what you’re talking about. I reached out before I bought two years later, right. I initially was going to do the installation. I, for some variety of reasons, two or three years, didn’t do the installation. And I went back to the company that had maintained the relationship and had connected me with the information I needed to make a good decision. And I didn’t get a quote from a single one of their competitors. Didn’t price shop. I’ve never looked around after that. So my first recommendation to you is, do not assume that user behavior is as cut and dry as you’ve described it. People make decisions in absolutely non-linear ways.

And I get it, lead gen, feels by definition, it’s linear, right. And it’s like, okay, someone’s, you know, it’s easy to look and say, okay, someone, you know, signing up to essentially a lead aggregator and those leads, it’s, you know, first come first serve, whoever picks up the phone first gets the, that is a segment of the audience, yes. I suspect a much larger segment of the audience. If for no other reason than the price point, right. Solar is not something in general, where someone has that money lying around on the top of their dresser.

And they’re like, okay, well, I’m gonna go, you know, let’s see what, I got three things on my to-do list today. You know, brush teeth, wash car, buy solar array. Like, that’s not how this works. Like this is an in-depth decision. So you can have a competitive advantage, simply by teaching people how to think about hiring the right installer. Like what do you know that they don’t? What questions should they ask first? How can you position yourself as the trusted guide who is not rushing them? Because everybody else in your industry is rushing, rushing, rushing ’cause they all have the same belief that you do, which is that it’s, you know, who gets there first gets the sale.

So everybody’s subject to that. And if you want to feel it, if you want to give the impression of being just like everybody else in their industry, then do it the way everybody else does. Or, the very first thing that you could do is say, listen, I understand that a purchase of this size is significant. So the first thing we’re going to do is we’re going to go you know, go through a, whatever. Over the next three days, I’m going to tell you how to think about this decision, the questions to ask everyone, the things that you’re not going to hear anywhere else. Right, now you have my attention. Be the voice that doesn’t sound like everybody else. Be the one that they want to actually look and say, who’s this? Because this seems different. It feels like a rant. Any thoughts on this?

[André] My only thought is one that, you know, we can only control what we can control. And thinking about or stressing over the things that we have no control over, doesn’t seem to be a valuable strategy. So, you know, if you’re in a market, people shop around, well, then you’re in a market where people shop around. But as Shawn said, there’s gonna be enough people that value relationships and when one is established and they, you know, through their relationship, some sort of bond is created.

People are weirdly, what’s the word? Once somebody likes somebody else, you know, that’s it, they’re going to choose that over somebody else where there’s no relationship. I’m sure there’s many markets where it’s very price sensitive. But, you know, this is expensive stuff. At least in the solar space. And I don’t know how competitive that is but I would have thought that all things being equal, you know, building and fostering good relationships, can’t be a bad thing.

[Shawn] Second part of this question is regarding the idea of serialized narratives. Should it start at the rebated SOI. Should it start at the sphere of influence or should it begin in the email series? So actually, for everyone who’s not familiar with sphere of influence, you know, the sphere of influence is built on this idea that there’s a, in general, a multi-page entry point, prior to opt-in, where we’re not offering a bribe, I’m using the quote, unquote ethical bribe, to get somebody to opt-in, that we’re taking a position, opening and closing loops, drawing attention forward so that the opt-in is part of a larger journey for the right prospects.

It’s an easy no for people who are not pulled forward. And an easy yes for people who want to learn more without the addition of some kind of bribe to get the email address. Because when we do that, we get a much muddier signal, than if it’s, sort of that pure, just intellectual interest or emotional desire. So, how do you want to think about this question André? I mean, serialized narratives in their relationship to spirit of influence. I just kinda answered it when I described it, so.

[André] Yeah. I mean, the sphere of influence inspired front-end is always gonna come first, you know. That’s where people have to get through before they can end up on an email list. So that context matters. It helps shape the quality and the frame that somebody has when they come through essentially, your front door, which is your opt-in. Which is why, you know, I’m not a fan of having these really short, blind opt-ins to make it really easy for somebody to get their free thing.

Because then you don’t really have the attention other than, you’ve delivered the thing that they’ve wanted or that they’ve come on here for. And once they’ve got their little free guide, then they’re off. And maybe you see them again and then maybe you don’t. So, you know, you can have the world’s best relationship build series narrative arc on there. And if somebody is just there to get something and then they’re off, you know, they’re off in the sense that, their attention is elsewhere. There’s nothing you can do to save that really. So, yeah, it all starts before somebody even gets onto your email list. But that’s a conversation for a completely different product.

[Shawn] Yeah and this would be our first reference to squirt of dopamine today, right. So one of the problems with the lead magnet is it’s an artificial squirt of dopamine that makes you feel better. Then you don’t have to do anything. You don’t have to consume it. You can go and you can read an opt-in page like, oh, there’s this, you’re gonna get this X, Y, Z video series, this and that is gonna change your world and on and on and on. Great and all I need to do is give you my email address?

Cool, I’ll give you my email address, boom, I got access to it, squirted dopamine. I don’t need to do anything now. There’s no more tension. I’ve got the thing, I can go check it out, whenever I want to, right. It’s so counterproductive. As opposed to generating excitement and the opt-in gets them to the next thing that they need to continue pulling their attention for, that’s opening even additional loops like you’re continuing, you’re not artificially closing attention or ending attention that a lead magnet doesn’t.

And people who download lead magnets. tend to be in Facebook. Well, certainly can confirm this. I mean, if you optimize for leads, you get lots of people who become leads and they’re not necessarily people who become customers. So anyway, we won’t go into that side, right. This next one out of Avaro. Oh, you’re gonna hate my answer to this question Avaro. Module two principles of serialized narratives.

This is not a question but at the end of the workshop you mentioned quote, one last thing before we wrap up today’s email, the most important lesson we shared today is hidden in plain sight. Once you see it, everything changes. It’s killing me, what do you mean? Sorry Avaro, you gotta go back and read it until you see it. Hidden in plain sight buddy. If we told you, that wouldn’t be any fun. It’s like the book, “Think and Grow Rich”, there’s like the secret of everything you need to know is in “Think and Grow Rich”, right.

I mean, it’s all there. You have to find it. And it has to be revealed to you, right. If someone tells you what it is, you’re like, whatever, that’s stupid. You have to have the experience of the revelation or it’s not meaningful for you. So if we tell you what it is, it’s not gonna be fun, sorry man. All right. You wanna twist the knife even further?

[André] I was just gonna say that you and I have both read a book that we really thoroughly enjoyed, I think it’s called “Wink and Grow Rich” or was there a different name for it now?

[Shawn] It’s “Wink and Grow Rich”.

[André] Wink and Grow Rich. And again, it’s the same thing. Like every single chapter is a different lesson you’ve got to figure it out for yourself. And then they all stack on each other. And I’ve read that maybe 10 times, I’ve got the audio book. So I’ve listened to it 10 times. And each time there’s something else.

But if somebody had just told me, like listed them all out in the beginning of the book, here, Chapter zero, here’s all the insights, that would have been the biggest waste of time. Because it would have had zero impact. You know, these things need to show up when they show up, which is why we reread things. And I think every single person on the call here, if they managed to reread, reconsume art of email, maybe each year, for 10 years, you’re gonna get new insights all day long. Yeah, that’s all I’m gonna say.

[Shawn] In a strange moment of serendipity, to the person who referred the book to us, to both of us, it’s his birthday today, isn’t that weird? Module three, soap opera series. This is Francis. Can you share your thoughts on using multiple themes in a single soap opera series. Like mixing good versus evil and us versus them. It feels like they could go well together. But also, I’m not sure if my understandings of themes is strong enough that I’m missing a glaring issue. I want you to tackle this one first.

[André] Yeah, I think we almost covered this on the very first question. I’m gonna stick to my answer and say, keep it simple and just have a single controlling idea. You know, this idea of a thesis or a theme, just keep it as one thing. My coach shared with me very early when we started our relationship and she shared this thing with me, which is a structure wheel. And in the center of the structure wheel, is this thing called thesis, your thesis statement, your controlling idea and everything, every single insight, every single thing that you wanna share, that then loops around this wheel, almost like the spokes of a wheel.

And then when it gets back to the top, then you would have finished the piece or the series or whatever it is. It’s the single idea that holds the whole thing together. I think it can get confusing when you’re trying to layer on too many of these things together. Or at least thinking of it that way. I think it’s fine to have these little sub-ideas but, you know, ultimately one series or one essay or one email needs to have a single, controlling idea. So this idea of a theme, just keep it as a single thing. And if other things show up, as you’re writing this thing within, then that’s fine. But ultimately they’re all attached to this one arc. This one thing that joins everything together.

[Shawn] Yeah, I think, I don’t know, I’ll disagree with you just for the sake of disagreeing. ‘Cause we never disagree. It’s not even disagreement, you know. In this example, good versus evil or us versus them, that maps perfectly. So sure, I mean but it’s not two themed, okay. Now I’m gonna agree with you again, dammit. It’s not two themes, it’s one theme. It’s, I’m good, they’re bad, right. That’s the theme. You know and sue it mixes good versus evil but you have to be able to say it in a sentence, right.

Like, what’s this whole thing about? It’s about A and B, right. What’s the art of email manifesto about? That conventional wisdom is wrong. And if you follow it, you’re going to get a suboptimal result. Like that’s not the copyrighting version. That’s what we’re saying, right. Now, is there a lot of nuance in that? Sure. You know, it’s wrong and because it’s wrong, you do things that make sense but they’re all downstream consequences of a bad assumption. So, you know, there’s layer upon layer but it all returns back to, you know, conventional wisdom, bad, way we do it, good, right. Us versus them.

Good versus evil. I mean, it’s there but we never sat down and wrote good versus evil or us versus them. And we never crafted it that way. We just crafted around that idea. And that’s how to think about theme. It’s just an idea. I mean, that’s all, I mean, it’s the thing that hangs the whole thing together. It’s the thing that the whole thing is about, right. What, like if someone says, what is this about? Whatever you say next, that’s the theme, right. That’s it. That’s the beginning and the end. Okay, next question. This is Mark. You’re gonna hate my answer to this question.

But I’m gonna let you answer this question first. ‘Cause I’m gonna sound like a jackass when I answer this question. We’ll do good cop, bad cop, all right. Here’s Mark’s question. If we’re opening and closing lots of loops across the sequence, how do we deal with people who don’t read every email? Which is most people. Do we just not worry about them because they’re not our most engaged people. This is about having links to earlier emails, so they can read ones they’d missed or is there something else we could deliberately do that people who dip into just one email still get the value? Okay, so question, if we’re opening, closing, lots of loops across the sequence, how do we deal with people who don’t read every email? André, good cop.

[André] I’ve got to be the good cop. Well, I mean, my first thing I’m gonna say is that we can’t force people’s attention, we can’t make people pay attention. We can only do our thing. And some people are gonna pay attention and some people aren’t. The whole idea of, you know, building and making an email list that is very targeted and there’s a strong relationship, means that by default, people are gonna be paying attention more often than not. But at the same time, attention is different to, I’m just gonna go on a slight tangent here quickly. The way that I see it is that attention and trust are two different things. So trust is something that you have.

It’s something that you build and you have a certain level of trust. And over time, that level of trust increases, just like a relationship with a friend who starts off as a stranger. And just because you don’t see that person every single day, there can be a whole year that goes by and you haven’t seen the person. But when you see the person, it’s like best friends again. Like you’ve just, you know, like the last time that you saw them was yesterday, not a year ago. So attention is kind of like an oscilloscope, it goes up and down because people have lives and you know, their attention will phase in and phase out.

This is one of the reasons why we do the whole bridge email thing. Is because it’s a mechanism to pull someone’s attention forward, for a certain journey. And those people that just don’t have the attention at that point, even though you have a strong relationship with them, you know, now, you know, when they faced with this thing, maybe now isn’t the right time. Their attention is elsewhere, something’s happened. They’ve lost a job or whatever. So those people typically won’t respond and therefore you don’t have their attention. So by creating these mechanisms that focus people’s attention, you’re more likely to have their attention.

And then, so now that you know, that you’re more likely, you’ve done something that’s increased your chances, that you’re gonna have the attention of most people in this pool, you know, then you use your open loops. I wouldn’t throw a kitchen sink of open loops in a single email because I think that can be counterproductive. But an open loop, you know, like a single powerful open loop, I think is more powerful than, like a whole bunch of them. Because then it just gets messy, you know. Your mind can’t. But if this is one thing, like the previous question that we, who was it from, about the module two, you know, where we mentioned at the end, that the insight is gonna be obvious when you see it, you know, that’s a single open loop that we put at the end and it’s driving that person dilly. I’m sure it’s driving many people insane.

But it’s not a smorgasbord of open loops. It’s just that one, strategically placed at the end. That’s gonna drive enough people insane, that they’re gonna reread that lesson, I don’t know how many times. So I’ll stop there for now. And I’m quite curious to see what the bad cop component is gonna be.

[Shawn] I’m just trying to get over the fact that you said that awareness is like an a oscilloscope. What the hell are you? What? I had to go Google oscilloscope while you were talking, to figure out what you meant. So I’m not trying to be bad cop to you but that is kind of funny. All right. So here’s the question for opening and closing lots of loops across the sequence, how do we deal with people who don’t read every email? My answer is, right better emails.

I mean, it sounds like the most obvious answer in the world and I’m not trying to be a jackass but like, that’s the deal, right. You gotta figure out the thing that like, grabs people by the throat and grabs their attention. That actually matters. Because if you’re not talking about the things that actually matter, people will ignore it. We all would. I mean, there’s no open loop in the world that’s gonna pull somebody’s attention towards something they don’t give a shit about, right. It’s just not possible, right. The thing you’ve gotta figure out, is what keeps this person up at night?

Like, what’s the thing that if they could solve right now, would meaningfully change their life? Like significantly change their life. Because if you’re talking about that, then you’re going to have their attention from beginning to end. And if you’re talking about that and they’re not paying attention, then they’re never going to pay attention, right. They clicked on it for, who knows why. But the people who are getting your emails, if you’re speaking to the thing that keeps them awake at night, that matters more to them than just about anything else, that if they could just solve it, their life would be infinitely better, finally. You talk about that, you don’t have to worry about anything else. So that’s the long answer. All right, next question.

[André] That’s one of the reasons why we’ve made the audience and off master class free for any customer of ours because it’s so important. All that stuff that you learn. If you actually did that work, would inform lots and lots of downstream ideas, yeah.

[Shawn] And it’s shocking. I mean, it’s shocking why people buy things. It’s never why we think people buy things, ever. There’s so much nuance to it, when you peel back those layers of nuance and you speak to that thing, that’s really driving the action, then you’ll have the right people’s attention. You won’t have everybody’s attention because everybody’s not paying attention at any moment in time, even anyone who raises their hand, right. You know, all right, let’s go on to the next one.

There’s so many questions. Okay. You say email frequency can be adjusted to match your needs and the needs of your audience. Do you have any suggestions for determining an audience’s preference for email frequency? When I’ve asked directly in the past, when I’ve reviewed unsolicited comments that people send me, I end up with a huge range of conflicting responses. Okay. André, anything on email.

[André] I think the first answer with that is to be careful what you ask for.

[Shawn] Yeah, never ask a question you don’t want an answer to, that’s rule number one.

[André] Yeah, I think a different way of asking a question like that would be, do you want the weekly summary or do you want the whole thing? You know, something like that. So it’s a this or a that. And then you’re acknowledging that certain people have different levels of attention and a once a week, you know, so the once a week summary would be every Saturday they click yes for that because that’s, you know, they’re busy and that sounds about right.

But when it’s too, like, do you want it daily or, you know, every third day or, then it just gets a bit out of hand and you’re gonna get the whole spectrum of people, you know, having their opinion. So I think that’s the quick answer.

[Shawn] Yeah. And I think, you know, the way I would tackle this is, what do you mean when you ask for email frequency? What emails, right. Is it a newsletter? Well, I mean, every week, every two weeks, every month, whenever the hell you feel like it, that makes sense, right. I mean, depending on what you’re giving to your audience. If it’s a mini course where, you know, someone needs to understand something and they need to sit down every day for 15 minutes. And if they do that over a course of five days, that time is going to give them a worthwhile result, then it needs to be daily.

You can let your audience know too, that listen, I’ve chosen the, you know, I wouldn’t use this language but you know, basically say, I’ve chosen the frequency for this series the way I have because it’s, from my perspective, knowing what I know, it’s the best way for you to understand it, internalize this information, right. I could tell this to you over the course of a month, once a week but that would not have the same impact if I do this. If you sit down for 15 minutes a day, over the next four days. Like that’s when you’ll get the most from it. And tell your audience that.

Don’t assume that they know why they’re getting things at the frequency. And those frequencies, you know, they undulate, which would probably show up under oscilloscope. I think, isn’t that true? Isn’t there undulation on an oscilloscope André? So what I mean by that is, you’re not a metronome of communication. Every 72 hours you get a thing from me. It can be more intense for a short period of time. Longer durations for other periods of time. And then there’s an ebb and a flow to everybody’s attention. And it really depends on what value you’re creating for your audience and how that value needs to be delivered and consumed by them. And you can tell them that on the front end.

So there’s no canonical right answer here. It is much more a sense of like, what’s the best way for somebody to understand this information that I’m sharing with them, to get the insights that I want them to have. Can they do that if they get this over four weeks or is it better if they do that all at once? And if it is over longer periods of time, then it probably makes sense to refer back to previous ones. So you can always orient people in space or you can time or you can also do that in the beginning. You see us do this a lot beginnings of emails as we’ll talk about, like this summer, when we do the four part courage and perseverance series.

We would start each of those parts, number two, three and four, by orienting to what, sort of where we were. So lots of ways to do this. Okay, next question. How would you approach things if you suspect your audience prefers shorter emails? I just want to say like find a new audience but I’m not gonna be that guy. Split things out into more emails, so each individual email is shorter. Leave longer gaps between emails, they have time to digest. Make the emails as long as they need to be. Get across your full message and assume that the right people will read all the way through, even though others don’t. So André, how would you approach things, if you suspect your audience prefer shorter emails?

[André] You know, I think again, there’s no, I mean, for us, there’s a reason why we’re not on Twitter for example. I’m gonna go on a small rant here. There’s a reason why we’re not on Twitter, is because people that enjoy snackworthy content, hang out on Twitter, for example. People that perhaps, given the choice would rather read a 200 word email as opposed to a 1500 word or a 1000 word email.

You know, we prefer, for our, you know, our audience. We prefer them to enjoy or demand deeper thoughts, you know. Which you can only unpack, you know, in a longer piece, you know, so. I think this is us and this is how we show up and this as part of our relationship building series. As you know, this is how we write our emails. This is how we show up. If this is too much for you, if it’s too much information, too deep, then, you know, unsubscribe but this is it.

So I think establishing those ground rules early on, if you have an audience that likes short things and you give them short things, then everyone’s gonna be happy. But if you’re unhappy with the results you’re getting from an audience, then perhaps you need to change it up and then set those, ground rules out early on. So then people know what to expect. And that’s it.

[Shawn] Yeah, I think my first response to this is now a 180 degrees different. So my first response is, you know, find a different audience, right. Like that’s glib. And I think that’s just me being an ornery dumb ass at nine o’clock in the morning. And if your audience prefers, I mean, first of all, how do you know? Is the first question? Don’t assume that your audience, then don’t ask them, hey, would you like a shorter email? People don’t know what they want.

I mean, you have no idea what we want. And what if he was to say, oh, I prefer shorter emails but then, you know, it’s the same person who reads 1500 words beginning to end at the edge of his or her seats. So we don’t generally know, so don’t ask your audience if they prefer shorter emails, observe their behavior and see what they seem to respond to. And if you want to write a short email, write a really good short email, right. One open loop, one, you know, one clear call to action. Something, give your audience something to do, right.

Don’t just hand them a, here’s the thought for today, go forth and conquer. The world is littered with garbage emails like that. Have something that’s interesting, that captures their attention as a new way to see the world and leave them with an action step to take. Like, you know, do this thing today. Or do this thing as a result. Or ask them a question or something. I mean, you don’t have to write 1500 words but there probably is a minimum at which, you need enough structure there to make it worthwhile. Or, the email, you know, the short email you prefer to read becomes the short email that you never read because there’s no value in it, right.

Short and value tend to go hand in hand in some weird curve. Too short, not enough value. Too long, too hard to extract the value. Somewhere in there there’s a sweet spot for you and your audience. It doesn’t have to be 1500 words, could be six or 700 words. But it needs to be enough that you’re bringing their interest back for more. And it’s really easy to think that you can shorten, shorten, shorten and you’re doing somebody a favor by doing that. But I don’t think that’s true. Focus on the value and then value dictates how long the email should be.

All right. Okay, these are three questions here. Let’s see, I don’t know. I’ll do these separately, I guess. Do you ever think of the soap opera series mapping onto the stages of awareness or any other high level concept like that? André, do you ever, in the last 27 years, thought about mapping a soap opera series to the stages of awareness?

[André] I’m a simple person. That’s way beyond my pay grade.

[Shawn] I mean it could, right. I mean, sure, I mean, you know. And for everyone who’s not familiar with the stages of awareness, you know, five stages of awareness, completely unaware, like someone who has no idea, you know, there’s even a problem or that there are solutions to the problem. Yeah there’s, problem awareness or solution awareness and it gets increasingly sophisticated. And you can imagine it left to right.

You know, and the less aware an audience is, the harder they are to convert, which is not rocket science, if you think about it. Yeah, there’s a sweet spot in there somewhere. But it’s really who are you talking to? Right and how are you generating traffic? And, you know, there, from my perspective, that, you know, trying to go after completely unaware audiences, makes no sense.

[André] It’s dangerous.

[Shawn] I call that a small fortune business. You know, if you start with a large fortune, you’re going to end up with a small fortune. But yeah, sure. If it’s a helpful thinking tool. Stages of awareness, I like, just from the perspective of, first it’s almost a check on my thinking, like, am I talking to people who, you know, Seth Godin talks about this, we’re not trying to convert the unconverted or the uncommitted, right. We’re not trying to make some. And I think it’s a Seth Godin example.

And I love this example, like, if you’re going to open a nudist colony, don’t try to convince people to become nudists, just find people who are already nudists and tell them about your nudist colony, right. Like that’s, to me, that’s all the wisdom you need in marketing in one sentence. Like, don’t try to go out into the world and convince people who know nothing about what it is that you’re talking about to care about it. I mean, you can, it’s just hard work.

I mean, I’d rather look for people who are already interested in what it is that we’re talking about in they’re engaged. And then send them to your nudist colony. And we’ll just keep the metaphor. Just to be clear, we don’t have a nudist colony, nobody needs that. All right. Next question. Starting from scratch, do you no more. I don’t understand this question. In starting from scratch with email marketing, how do you determine which soap opera series are best to start with? André, you should answer this one.

[André] Yeah, I don’t know, there’s no single answer for any of these. You know, for questions like that.

[Shawn] I wonder if Joe meant which pockets of people are best to start with, right? I mean, why would you, if you’re starting? Well, no, maybe not. If you’re starting from scratch with email marketing, so they do not even have a list. So you don’t even have a list then you don’t need a soap opera series yet, right? I don’t think I understand the question. If you’re with us, is Joe with us?

Joe, Joe’s not with us. Joe, when you hear this recording or we’ll reach out to you, Nick, if you could reach out to Joe and just ask him what he meant, like, question number two, I don’t think we understand it. We’ll move on to question number three. Do you find different soap opera series containers, for example, mini courses, first person stories, case studies, fit best for different stages? For example, first person stories are better early in their journey. Case studies are better, leading to the sale. What do you think André?

[André] I don’t know if I have a have a good answer for that. I think, you know, case studies are great when you’re heading towards a sale, when you’re using other people’s to demonstrate that they’ve been able to, you know, implement this thing and get a result.

You’re shining a light on their story. I think that’s fine. But, you know, containers, mini courses I think is a fantastic way to essentially frame people coming into your world from the outside. So instead of having a lead magnet, you have a mini course. I think that’s more exciting ’cause it’s not something you can just give somebody and then they’ve got it.

And then they can wonder off. It’s through that mini course journey. They, you know, all sorts of magic can happen. You know, I used to write first person stories all the time because it was just me, you know. Now it’s Shawn and me. And so everything’s not first person anymore. Yeah.

[Shawn] I don’t have a great answer to this. I mean, I think I love the mini course because essentially a mini course is a way to create good customers and get rid of people who are not going to be good customers. You know, I love the mini course idea, simply because it’s a way to say, listen, in order to understand what I’m want, you know, you’re not saying this overtly. But it’s basically like, in order to really understand what I’m going to show you eventually what you can buy, you need to understand these things.

And this needs to be like your default operating system by the end of this experience. And once you’ve internalized those ideas and the things I’m going to talk about later, are the natural evolution of those things that I’ve now shown you, matter, right. So, I mean, again, we use the art of email manifesto. If you read the art of email manifesto and not alphabetical son of a gun, these guys got it, they figured it out.

Conventional wisdom makes me do things that make perfect sense if conventional wisdom were right but because it’s wrong, they make no sense and they’re actually counterproductive. When you’re nodding your head up and down at the end of that, then the of art of email is a logical next step for you. It’s very clear. I’m not suggesting this is transactional. So it’s like, okay, you know, whatever, soap opera series, now I need you to buy immediately after. But it needs to be coherent in some way. And people will respond to first person stories.

And you can do a first person story, that’s a mini course. You can live vicariously through somebody else. So these aren’t mutually exclusive. Just comes down to like, what does somebody need to internalize? What do they need to accept, believe, internalize, to the value that you create for them is the logical conclusion of that worldview, right. Like that’s, that’s it. Okay.

[André] Yeah.

[Shawn] Next question.

[André] I think mini courses is a higher order idea that I like to think about. And then within that, you know how you want to present your point of view, that’s the first person piece, or, you know, then it doesn’t have to be third person. You can break into third person if you’re sharing a case study. And case studies can be injected within these containers. It’s not necessarily its own thing. So, you know, the point of view and case studies can be used inside of things like mini courses.

I think Jonathan Baker, I think we had a series last year where we drew attention to him, I think we interviewed him too, I can’t remember.

[André] Yes we did.

[Shawn] But to me, Jonathan Baker was like, he’s a perfect example of how this idea works. So Jonathan Baker works with elite cyclists to go faster on a bicycle, essentially. I think he has a mini course. But the framing is very clear. And it’s so compelling. The framing, you know, he has so much expertise in this field. And he basically said, there are three ways to go faster on a bicycle, you can improve the aerodynamics, reduce the weight or generate more power. You know, improving the aerodynamics and shaving off weight. are very expensive, low impact solutions. If you want to go faster on a bicycle, you need to generate more power.

And that’s what Jonathan teaches cyclists. He creates custom training plans for people to generate more power, to go faster on a bicycle. I mean, that to me, it’s such a perfect example of framing, right. It’s an expert saying to you, if you’re trying to do X, here are the three ways that you can do X. Two of them don’t make any sense once I explain them to you. You spend a ton of money for a wind tunnel And it’s not gonna really help. You can, you know, spend thousands of dollars for wheels and shave off, you know, grams of weight.

But you’re not really going any faster. If you wanna go faster, you have to generate more power. And that’s what he spent a career, you know, working with cyclists to generate more power on the bicycle. It’s so brilliant because by the time you’re exposed to his messaging, you reach the inescapable conclusion, that if you want to go faster on a bicycle, first of all, A, you need to generate more power. And B, Jonathan has spent a career training people how to do that. So you’re clearly in the right place. That’s the simple version. But that’s an empire building version of this idea. So, all right. Next question.

[André] Yeah and then, you know, Jonathan’s gonna talk in his first person point of view. But then he can also break into the customer case studies, where he’s, you know, let me tell you about Frank. And then he’ll break into third person within that narrative. So you can mix and match these things. And by doing that, it creates this very compelling narrative that’s gonna maintain someone’s attention all the way through.

[Shawn] Yeah, that model works really well. Just for everybody to think about this. That model works exceptionally well across, I think any market. If you can orient to somebody, think about the benefit of this whole structure. Number one, to be able to say to somebody, this is what you want. This is what you’re trying to accomplish. And then to say and these are the X number of ways that you can get from where you are to there. And here are all the ways that don’t make any sense, for these reasons.

And this is what does make sense. That’s what experts do. I think I’ve called this in the past, like the expert frame. And you don’t have to be capital E expert. You just have to know what you’re talking about enough to say all of those things. Now in doing that, it’s such a gift for the person who’s experiencing it to feel like, okay, someone’s sort of explained this whole thing to me. I get it now.

Like this is the high level view that they’ve indicated where I’m trying to go and I’m like, yep, that’s where I’m trying to go. They’ve shown me the ways I could get there. And I’ve heard about a lot of these things in the past. And they’ve also shown me like where each, you know, each one makes sense and where they don’t and now, you know. And yes, of course the thing that you’re recommending, is the thing that you do. But you’ve made a really compelling case for it. And it makes sense. And that’s very, very, very powerful.

Especially when you’re inside a topic that somebody doesn’t know something about, you can show them what it looks like inside the tent. Okay. Avaro asked the question. There’s a lot of background with this but I don’t think we need the background. So the question is this. How can we avoid a situation where a person goes through different soap opera series simultaneously? I assume there’s a technical answer to this question.

[André] Yeah, well, it’ll almost be impossible from the sense that, you know, a soap opera series is something that gets triggered within the context of some of something else. Of a sequence that someone’s going through or through a newsletter, there’s a link inside there. I mean, they can click on that single link multiple times. It’s not gonna restart their progress inside of it. But, you know, there’s no other soap opera series for them to click on.

[Shawn] Let me read the rest of it to give you some context, so.

[André] Okay.

[Shawn] I can understand where this could go wrong. So let’s say for the sake of argument, I’ll summarize what you said. Let’s say for the sake of argument that someone gets to the end of our relationship building series and we have a, choose your own adventure email, where you can say, hey, if you’re interested in generating more awareness for your business, you know, X, Y, Z and engagement and conversion.

And someone says, oh, hell, yeah, I want to know about awareness. And they click on something that triggers the soap opera series for the traffic engine and then they’re looking at their email and they’re like, oh, hell, not only that, I want to understand conversion too because those two things are super important. Then they click on the link for the soap opera series for, like, how do we manage? Like, you know. And I’m gonna assume this is just a tag based thing. Like something starts something ends, you can’t do two things simultaneously or can you?

[André] Yeah. I mean there’s multiple ways to approach this. And, you know, there’s maybe a better way, maybe not. But this really depends on how you choose to do this. And obviously communicate what’s gonna happen inside the body of the email that’s framing this, choose your own adventure. So at the end of a relationship building series, for example, if you decide to do, a choose your own adventure and there’s three options, I would probably use a custom field. So it can only have a single value.

And based on that, it’ll only send them one thing. So you can communicate that, you know, choose one thing here. You can’t choose multiple ’cause it’ll just select the last thing that you’ve chosen. So you can say, just pick one of these three, later there’ll be an opportunity to go through another one. So that’s one way to do it. You could also use tags and have a tag trigger one of these things.

But then again, just communicate that. If you’re interested in all three of these things, you’re gonna be getting three emails every single day for a week. So I think that’s one way to do it. We’re not gonna be prescriptive and tell you which way you should choose. That’s just, you know, up to how you decide that you want somebody to interact with you. If multiple emails, every single day, is gonna be overwhelming, then just rig it in such a way that they can only choose one thing.

[Shawn] I don’t have very much to add to this other than, you know, I wouldn’t overthink it. I think of my own buying behavior, if I click on three things, you send me three emails every day. I’m okay with that. I understand the logic of one thing at a time but when someone is indicated, I think a baseline principle that I live by, is that people vote with their behavior, right. So when someone stands up and says, you know, send me the info about convert. And maybe they don’t know what they’re about to get into. But, I don’t know, I think if someone says, hey, tell me about all of this stuff. Like they just entered our world and they’re gung-ho. And like, I want to know about it all, click on it all, send it all, hold my beer, watch this shit.

Like, that’s my, I don’t know. That’s kind of a bad answer but. I don’t think there’s a right answer here. Okay. Module four relationship building series. Francis asked the question. Do you recommend one relationship building series that everyone sees when they start. Or should there be one for each core offer? I think we have about 275 variations of this question. So this is an important one. You know, Francis says we have several courses. Each can stand alone to be enough, similar to your set up. They’re also fairly different, kind of complimentary.

Someone purchasing one course can easily pick up another and add that service to their business. They can also only be interested in one specific service with zero interest in any others. I’ll give André a second to think about this one. And I’ll just do a quick answer. So, first of all, simple, first like, you know, there’s a saying, I think it’s from the theory of constraints, if you’re gonna march elephants down a hallway, do it one at a time, right. So don’t try to write for relationship building series simultaneously because, then you’re just, you’re not going to create any value until every single one of them is complete, right. One relationship building series.

Figuring out whichever one’s most valuable to you. Now, do you recommend one for everyone or for each core offer? That really depends on several factors. How are you generating traffic? If you’re generating traffic at the product level, then you should have relationship building series related to that product. If people are raising their hand to indicate interest at the product level, then yes, you need a relationship building series at the product level. If instead, your people are aware of you as like a group of experts and they wanna have kind of a sense of, you know, how to make a decision about the products and you need to educate them before they even know what they’re looking for, then yes, you have one relationship building series, that kind of gives them an overview.

That’s what we do. You know, we kind of say, this is what we believe and here’s our core principles. This is why we think they’re important. And the end of the discussion of our core principles, that’s the point at which we begin to talk about the fact that we do have some courses. But that’s after we have let you know where we stand on some things. Because the courses don’t make sense out of that context. However, if we decided to generate traffic at the product level, then we would have a relationship building series at the product level. And we would say, you know, we just talked about this recently, you know, something recorded but that, yes, it was in the workshop, week two, content week. This is one of the things we discussed. That you can have an entry point into your world that is a relationship building series that focuses more on the value the product offers, versus the people behind the product. Any thoughts to add to that one, André?

– Yeah. I think, you know, all things being equal, you would probably want to have a relationship building series attached to every single product’s front door. And also at a more general one. The way that we get around it, because, as Shawn said, you know, it requires a lot of bandwidth to be able to write multiple of these things. If you’ve got five products, then that’s a lot to write.

So one way to get around that is, so I’ll ping a link into, I’ve targeted everybody, so it doesn’t just go to myself and Shawn. So that’s the link to the durable business product page, some of you have seen it and some of you haven’t. And you’ll notice that, when we did a PLS last year I think it was, all of those emails are directly accessible on that page. So that’s allowed us not to, you know, not to have to have a relationship building series opt-in on that page. Someone can land on that page and they can browse around.

They can see all our old emails. All from that single page. So they can have a similar experience without having to opt-in. And then they’ll come in through our general list because all roads lead to that on our site currently. So that may be an intermediary way of doing it. But, you know, ultimately I think we would want to have a RBS on every doorway in. Regardless of the stuff that we have on the product page.

You know, all of these assets that we put on our pages expand our world. So our building efforts, attract people in their rank in the search engines. People come in, they land up on a page, that’s perhaps a sub-page of a product page and they browse around and they read this thing and it sucks them in. And they’ll, you know, at some point they’ll be ready to opt-in. Maybe it’s for that product. Maybe it’s, they’ve now just been expose to a whole world of stuff that wasn’t even in their awareness. And now they’re on this amazing journey.

So, in that respect, world-building is very non-linear. And I know that wasn’t your question directly but all of this is, you know, comes from world-building. So we have the world-building on our website. And then when somebody is opting in and they’re raising their hand to say that they want a bit more of this thing and that’s just a deeper level. And then the world-building comes online, you know, comes into someone’s inbox. So you can do it in these different layers. Yeah.

[Shawn] I’ll move on to the next question. Wolf says, what happens if someone unsubscribes during the relationship building series, do you still subscribe them to the newsletter? Absolutely not. Someone unsubscribes they’ve unsubscribed. Now, I suppose you could have a link at the top that says, hey, you’re getting this relationship building series and you can unsubscribe from it or there’s a full nuclear unsubscribe in the footer.

But, we don’t do that. If someone doesn’t go through the relationship building series, if they unsubscribe at that point, they’re gone. And that’s fine. And I mean, we’re not going to make everybody happy. If someone tells you, again, people vote with their behavior. If you’ve said, this is what you need to understand to be a good citizen in my world, right. To care about what I care about. To see the value in what I’m creating. And it’s, you know, five emails, six emails and, you know, sort of walk you through it.

And someone’s like, screw this, I don’t like this. Why would you want them to stay, right. Like, thank them, you know? And you know, no one’s bad, no one’s good. They’re just not your people. And they let you know. And thanks. Next question. If someone resubscribes down the road, would you send the RBS again? I say, yes, André, what are your thoughts on these two questions?

[André] Yeah. So I’m just trying to think of what happens if somebody unsubscribes, they’re still on your list but they get made inactive. I don’t think they can resubscribe again at that point. Because they’ve done the full nuclear bomb, unsubscribed. So although they exist as a lead inside of ConvertKit, for example, they’re kind of grayed out and we have to reach out, we have to manually reach out to the folks at ConvertKit and ask them to reactivate that account.

We can delete that lead and then ask them to resubscribe. And then they’ll come in as a fresh new person, again, with the same email address. But it’s normally an extra step for us, which we do because, you know, sometimes someone will we’ll reach out and said, hey, I think I unsubscribed at some point and I’m trying to resubscribe again but I’m not getting your emails. And that’s the reason why I presume or I’m just assuming that maybe not all ESPs choose to operate in the same way.

Maybe they do. Maybe there’s some law saying that if somebody unsubscribes, then there needs to be a proper and valid reason for them to resubscribe again, I don’t know. But with ConvertKit I’ve got to reach out to support and ask them to reactivate an account, at that point.

[Shawn] Move on to the next question. This is Karma. When someone is either in your relationship building series or any other soap opera series, do they also get the newsletter or do you delete their newsletter tag while they’re inside the soap opera series and reapply it at the end? I think we covered this somewhere but why don’t you go? And this is sort of technical stuff, so.

[André] Yeah, when they first come into our world and they receive an RBS, at that point, they don’t receive anything else from us, other than, that obvious. And they’re only added to the newsletter tag at the end of that. Or, in some cases, if you choose to stack a few things together, so it’ll be an RBS.

If it’s a product specific RBS, so someone’s essentially raised their hand to receive something about a product and that’s essentially classed as, you know, the way that we see that is, that’s somebody raising their hand and saying, I want to know about this product. Then that RBS may be stacked with the story product promotion right afterwards. And then after that, we will then add them to the newsletter.

So again, it’s up to you but, we definitely don’t add somebody to the newsletter as soon as they join. Because there’s no relationship at that point. And then suddenly, maybe they opt-in on the same day that we send out a newsletter and that kind of, you know, that seems weird and jarring. So we let them wait for that. Then they get it.

[Shawn] All right. I’ll move on to the next question. Also from Karma, there’s a little background on this. It’s a really interesting question for any service providers out there. So the question is, how would you go about building a relationship building series and a soap opera series for a client when you’re not an expert in that field? In the background info, he has a client that’s a master at Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu. He does their marketing and advertising. And Karma mentioned that he knows Jiu-Jitsu pretty well but he’s not an expert. So how do you write, you know, an AOE inspired email series, if you’re not an expert?

So, I’ll jump on this one just because I’m familiar with Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu and some of the ways to describe, to get people to be interested in it. So, the first question you have to ask yourself is, who’s the audience that you’re writing for and what do they care about? Because most likely, I mean, if this is an instructor who is trying to generate students locally, they don’t care about anything that the level of mastery, they’re not really, you know, most of the people who are coming are going to be parents that were bringing their kids.

So you’re speaking to, sort of the higher level view of, you know, what you Jiu-Jitsu, you know, what martial arts in general do for kids, for as far as self-esteem and other things, there’s plenty of data out there about that anecdotal and otherwise. Easy to get parents to say, you know, little Joey came in and he was timid and this and that and the other. Since he’s been working with master so-and-so, he’s a totally different kid. I mean, martial arts, transform kids. I know that, I was 12 when I started Tae Kwon Do.

I’ve seen it happen hundreds, if not thousands of times in my lifetime, I know that I know it’s true that martial arts completely transform kids and parents care about that. So, you have to know the audience. Now, if this person is selling, like Jiu-Jitsu instruction online to high level practitioners, it’s a very different audience with a very different set of needs. And then it’s not so much about the act, it’s about positioning this person, you know or how he or she can be valuable to that audience. That’s an entirely different approach. You don’t have to be an expert in that.

So once you understand who the audience is, you need to understand what matters to that audience. And most likely, you’re going to need to get some of that information from the client, right. So if it’s the first audience and their kids, it would be primarily parents bringing kids, then you just need to ask the question, you know, ask this instructor, what do you think the benefits are to kids? Now, he or she may not know, right. I mean, I’ve been around plenty of martial artists in my lifetime and they don’t always know, you know, they just, they did the thing and now they teach the thing and whatever, some of them know, right.

The danger is getting it wrong, right. Like one of the most compelling arguments for Jiu-Jitsu, you hear this over and over and over again is, you know, 85, the numbers change. But it’s usually some variation of 85% of fights thrown to the ground. And Jiu-Jitsu is a martial art that emphasizes ground fighting. So, now therefore Jiu-Jitsu’s the best thing ever. Everyone’s gonna go to the ground anyway, you might as well know something, be comfortable fighting on the ground. But that that’s not relevant to the mom or dad, thinking about bringing their kid to train in Jiu-Jitsu. It can be completely true. That’s not what they care about.

They don’t want to think about little Johnny floundering around on a playground in a fight with somebody. They want little Johnny to feel confident enough that he’s not fighting at school. He’s not getting picked on at school. Like that’s part of it. And another part of it is that, you know, there’s a certain broad self-esteem that comes from training in the martial art, right. There’s just all kinds of benefits.

So focus on the benefits to the audience who you’re speaking to. The instructor doesn’t have to write, that instructor probably is not the person to write that, you might need to do some research. Start with the audience, forget what matters to the audience, write that and get the instructor to kind of look at it. And if you were the instructor or getting in the weeds of Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu mastery in your emails and you’re not speaking to an audience that really cares about mastery of Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu at the technique level, you’ve missed the mark. Because most audiences don’t care about that. That’s way too advanced at that point. Then again, it’s without knowing what the audience here is on through flying blind. That’s how would think about this. André, anything to add on that?

[André] Yeah, I’ll only add that, for many years, people that have reached out to us and have asked, do you guys write to sequences for clients? And it’s always no. And for years I’ve been referring people to a dear friend of us, Sue Russ. And so she writes emails for clients. She does this huge amount of research.

I’ve seen some of it, I’ve got a glimpse into the level of research she does. And it’s ridiculous. And she does that because, then she can write the best emails she possibly can for that client. So I think that’s just a more general response that, you know, there’s a lot of research that should happen in order to be able to write world-class emails and certainly a world-class email sequence. Yeah, so, just, you know, do a bunch of research.

[Shawn] Yeah. And that’s the secret you hear from every single copywriter. I mean, if you, you name like, really legitimate copywriters, what they’ll tell you, it’s not the writing, it’s the research. We’re not suggesting copywriting. It’s just, it’s an example, right. But if your livelihood depends on writing well to sell and overwhelmingly, what you spend your time doing is research, that’s a good indicator of where time is best spent.

It’s really hard to write something when you don’t really know what you’re writing about. It tends to be obvious And we’ve all been exposed to that kind of writing. So, you know.
