And a lot of it is a winnowing down process to like ask, interview the instructor and say, let’s just for the sake of argument assume that this instructor is trying to bring students in. 80% of the students who come in are kids. Everybody else is, you know, sort of, is adults. Start with the kids. Ask the instructor, why do you think parents bring their kids here? What have you heard over the years? Make a list. Prioritize that list. Further confirm it, if possible. Talk to people who are there.

Like, talk to the parents, if you can go there to the training area. Sit there and ask questions of the parents who are sitting there when their kids are training. You know, why did you bring little Johnny here? Why did you bring Jane here? Like, whatever, just get their information. Like do the work and the emails will write themselves. Okay. Next question is from Guillermo Am I correct in interpreting the RBS sequences and soap opera relationship building series and soap opera series, along with a couple other parts of art of email, are multi-part purpose frameworks or vehicles?

Meaning, you have all kinds of them. What they are used for depends on what the end goal is. You know, an RBS is to build a relationship not to sell on it. But it can be used to build relationships from different angles, even for different products or services in your business. Okay. Does that make sense, André?

[André] Yeah, I think so. I think the answer’s affirmative. I think he’s understood that, unless I’m.

[Shawn] The answer is yes. That is the correct interpretation. That’s an easy answer, all right. We’ll move on to the next one. So when it comes to getting people in your art of email style world for a new product or course, what is the best strategy or vehicle tool that you recommend? So he says, I’m talking zero lists complete, cold, no audience.

I own all your products. What is the best practice to get people into an us? So this is a list building question. I don’t know, do want to tackle a list building question?

[André] I mean, we can’t tackle it to the level that would be useful so, I don’t.

[Shawn] There are three free courses on the site, on the learn section. One’s on paid traffic. One is on list building and one is on email. I would go read the list building one first and then the paid traffic one second. That’s really the answer to this question. All right, question number three. Would you say that when delivering value before they become a customer, you’re describing the why behind the product. And then after they buy the product, they’re getting to how to use it?

[André] I guess so. I mean, I can only presume that maybe he’s overthinking this. I don’t know but, you know, you can’t explain how to use something if somebody’s not a customer, you know, you can’t explain the nuances of building a soap opera series to put someone that’s not a customer right, of art of email. Unless that’s what we want to do. Unless we wanna, as a free course, which we have on our site. That exact same thing, then we can show, some how to.

I don’t think how to has to live inside of, you know, behind something that’s paid. I think how to’s can, you know, our site, our blog is full of how to’s. So I think how to’s can be anywhere and why’s can be anywhere, you know. Unless I’m just not understanding the question clearly.

[Shawn] I’m looking at all three of these questions I think my observation, at least the first and the second one is that, this idea of distinctions that don’t make a difference. Right, like and I get it. I mean, I appreciate wanting to like, really understand the nuance here. But I think the answer is that, this isn’t where the nuance is, right. Because if we make it binary, we’re like, okay, so before someone becomes a customer, it’s about delivering value, based on the why behind a product. Well that eliminates, I mean, that eliminates so many opportunities, right.

I mean, sure, you can do that but you could also tell somebody about what it’s like when they do own the product. And you can talk about the product, why not? If that’s what makes sense. Like, if that’s, again, it gets back to this idea, what matters to the person reading it, like, what lights them up? And to have this false dichotomy. And this is a word of warning for everyone. Don’t make these distinctions to try to get to some, like, answers like, oh, it’s this.

There is no this, in the art of email. There is no this, in the real world. And there’s just like one thing like, oh, it’s about this. No, it could be any of those things, right. It comes back to this question, that when you’re building a relationship with somebody, what matters? In that conversation, what matters? Now if they’ve raised their hand and said, I’m interested to learn more about you and sort of the value you created. Oh okay, well there’s a conversation around, you know, here’s the sort of, my experience, my expertise, here’s how I think about things.

And from that, I have, you know, created a course or courses or a product or whatever it is, that expresses that value. Okay, that’s fine. Maybe somebody comes in and you’re less important than the overall thing because of the nature of your system, they come in and they’re interested in the course that you have. That solves a very specific need that they have. Then tell them about the course or the product, right. Just think about the context in which somebody shows up in your world and start the conversation there and don’t make a false dichotomy when that limits you, rather than expands.

We want every decision you make to give you a richer tapestry of options to choose from. Not to winnow down to a point where like, oh, okay, this relationship building series has these three ingredients, on this timeline, at this frequency. That’s far too prescriptive. That gets into the realm of templates. And there are no templates that work for everyone. And that’s why we don’t talk about templates. So, I wouldn’t keep looking for this type of answer, I guess is the way I would think about it.

Okay I don’t know what NBW is. Here’s the question. You can tell my brain’s just misfiring. He’s like, we use all these acronyms and I think I don’t know whatever acronyms means, okay.

[André] Network Building Workshop.

[Shawn] You think so?

[André] Yeah, Network Building Workshop.

[Shawn] That’s what I thought but I don’t, who knows. All right. This is from Brian. Does a landing page relationship building series example it’s in the and relationship building series in the course, okay. So does the RBS mentioned, qualify as a good momentum builder workshop example, minus that hard bridge. Okay, I have no idea what the hell this question is. Sorry, everyone, I’m trying to decipher this.

From the momentum builder workshop, I took that the relationship building series needed to leave stories about the business owner to build authority and not just information. However, the relationship building series from the module itself, seems more informational. According to what I’ve read in the art of email, we can use stories or informational content, right? As long as we take them on a belief journey into our world, our product is the next logical step. Is that the right way to look at it? Okay.

So just for everyone, in case anyone’s not familiar with it, MBW, momentum builder workshop, lead edition is a workshop that we did that, it’s really designed to, it’s really a, if we’re being honest, it’s an excuse elimination workshop, right. And what we mean by that is that, no matter what you do, it’s easy to have an excuse for why you’re not actually out in the world and making things happen, right. So you write a good email. You’re like, yeah, I don’t have a landing page yet, So I’m gonna do that. And it’s like, oh, you got a great landing page. And you know, I don’t have any traffic yet. What we did instead is we’re like, okay, here’s everything that you need, right.

You need one Facebook ad, one landing page, a relationship building series, a bridge and then a promotional series. Like that’s everything. Right. So that is a tiny, tiny, tiny, tiny slice of the art of email, right. That’s just a, the email component of that is a very thin slice of a way to go from beginning to end with no excuses, right. Five weeks, get something out in the world and then build on it. That’s entirely different than what we’re talking about in the art of email. Use the art of email to inform your work and the momentum builder workshop, don’t use the momentum builder workshop to inform your work in the art of email, that’s going in the wrong direction. If that makes sense. So, André, thoughts.

[André] Yeah, I don’t have anything to add to that, think you’ve nailed it.

[Shawn] I was just lucky. Alright. These are questions from Christian. It looks, we got quite a few here from Christian. All right. In the course you’ve mentioned that there are multiple ways into a relationship building series and also a multiple relationship building series. Just to clarify, there can be multiple ways. And there can be multiple RBS’s.

Doesn’t have to be. You did say around 2020, in the relationship building series video, that three pages was probably too short for a relationship building series. Is it okay to break that rule a little? So three emails. Okay, so André, do you think three emails is enough in a relationship building series? I think we said like four to nine.

[André] Yeah, I mean, I’m just reading something inside here, that he may not have said. But why would you want to? Why would you want to compact your ability to foster this new relationship with people coming into your world? Why is three better than five or nine? Unless you want to rush towards a sale? He’s not said that specifically but I’m just wondering why three? If you can create a relationship in just three emails, then try three emails, put it out there and see what happens, would be the quick answer.

But in our experience, less is not more, you know, as it relates to an RBS. You know, I think, when it’s a product centric RBS, you can get into talking about the actual product, you know, from email one, straight away. And then just allow the transition to happen towards a promotion, that can happen naturally. I don’t see why that needs to be squeezed into this idea of three. Yeah, that’s my response.

[Shawn] Yeah. I don’t know. But I think that’s the answer. Everything’s context and I know there’s a lot more context and I’m familiar with Christian’s business, so a message that’s affecting my thinking. I think we might come back to this when we get down to the rest of this. Okay. Here’s some background. So everyone’s on the same page with Christian and his business. My world revolves around the big idea that someone could become an efficient hunter by mixing lean manufacturing principles, with hunting skills and knowledge, the pre-sell sites, as an idea from serve influence, and the relationship building series together will be fairly content and idea heavy.

And I think that’s fine for organic traffic. For paid traffic, I think the framework is to idea rich. I think but I don’t know. I’m also judging based on how easy it is to write and unwrap ideas in a way that pulls but doesn’t overwhelm. When it’s someone whose first touchpoint is an ad, I think it’s easier to present parts of the concept separately, the small idea and ads and then tie them in with a short landing page in the mini relationship building series. Once someone has gone through a mini relationship building series, they will transition to the value newsletter. And I will also offer them the pre-sell site as a soap opera series, okay.

That way I feel easier to work with paid traffic and everyone gets the opportunity to consume the main relationship building series. I notice your entry to the relationship building series as a single landing page, not a more expensive pre-sell site. It’s two pages. Do you think it’s correct to use a bigger pre-sell site? When I have to explain more ideas? Probably be six to eight pages, when we’ve edited them down. Or have you gone away from the pre-sell site concept? Okay, there’s so, so, so much in this question, I’ll take a stab at it, André and then hand it off to you. Because I get this, I mean, I’ve run a little paid traffic in my lifetime and I get it.

And I’ve heard this argument a million times. I have seen evidence for it and seen evidence that contradicts it. But here’s the basic argument. You’re paying for traffic. You don’t have time for all of this relationship building stuff and all the, you know, the multi page pre-sales in the beginning and it’s just too complicated. Get them on the damn list. And then we’ll let the email do the work, right. Or get them to buy something and we’ll let the email do the work. That is the siren call of paid traffic. And sure, there’s certainly some evidence for that but life’s not that simple, right. And we’re talking about a significant spectrum of activity here.

So first of all. The very first question for paid traffic is, what is the intent? Is it active intent? They’ve got a bleeding neck search term and you’re using Google and they want something right away. And I’ll give them an eight page pre-sell site in a multi-part, you know, 10 entry relationship building series, right. And so we know if it’s Facebook and they’re not really looking and they stumbled onto you and they might be a good candidate based on the combination of demographic and psychographic factors.

And you’ve captured their interest in an ad, preferably enough content in the ad. It’s not just curiosity driven but they sort of looked at that and thought, oh, this is interesting enough to give some attention to it. And then, what they see next is a continuation of that, that the ad is framed for them. And that leads to a non-bribe situation, where they can learn more. It’s a lot of hurdles but the quality of that signal is extraordinary. Now, you have to find for your business, somewhere in there, that it’s the right balance of enough hurdles, so that you’re filtering out the wrong audience. And not so many hurdles that you’re just being gratuitous and a pain in the ass, right.

There is no right answer to that. But it’s not a question of, does this model with a pre-sell site in a relationship building series, only work for organic traffic and not for paid traffic. That’s demonstrably false. It absolutely can work with paid traffic. It may not work exactly the same way though. So, if a significant amount of your traffic comes, let’s just for the sake of argument, say it’s paid traffic and the platform is Facebook. There’s no canonical set of rules. It’s like, oh, because it’s this, we’re going to do it that way. You know, the ad is framing what happens next, right. That’s the way I think of it. Captures their attention. It sets a frame.

It opens, you know, it may open and close some loops within the ad itself to bring their attention through the consumption of the ad. The ad, in my opinion, is like a bouncer at a nightclub, right. It’s the person standing at the door. You can’t just walk in, right. There’s a little bit of work to do. And for the wrong people who are like, ah, I’m not interested. It doesn’t make any sense and they leave, good.

It’s a filtering tool. But some percentage of people, this is interesting, like, okay, this makes sense to me, let me find out more. The landing page that they go to, you know, that they came from Facebook, not because they were looking, they didn’t have active intent. They had passive potential interest. So knowing that framing, the question I would ask myself is, how do I capture somebody’s attention? Like what’s the length of time I have that is that balance between enough, where I’m weeding out the people who are just tire kicking.

They’re not interested at all. They’re in the lies at grocery counter. Or at grocery store, just trying to pass the time clicking on stuff. Versus the person who is engaged. Who’s reading it. And goes from at least one page to another or maybe not. Maybe you do a Jonathan Boyd style that we’ve talked about Johnson’s landing page at 1600 words, all on one page and you get to the end and you opt-in or not, right. Whatever that is, we can’t escape from the fundamental reality. And this to me is central to everything that we’re talking about. We can choose to change the system that we’re creating and try to get different segments of the audience and try to get people in our audience to do things differently. But at the end of the day, when you look across large sums of data, most people don’t buy immediately.

That is a fact of life, no different than gravity. It’s not our opinion. It’s not something that we think might be true. The evidence is overwhelming. Of all the people who are going to buy from you, the overwhelming majority of them are not going to buy from you in the first 90 days. That’s fact. So we could either try to just optimize our business for the people who are going to buy at 90 days. And make a business out of that. That’s one choice. Or we can optimize our business so that we get those people who are going to buy anyway at the 90 days. And we get everybody else. That’s what should drive our decision-making. So every question that we come back to, we want to come back to this, like, what is it we’re optimizing for here? Like, are we trying to get two or three more people out of a 100 to buy right now?

And if we do that, at what cost? Is it the cost of the people? The people who we turn away, who might not buy later now, like that’s the trade-off. So, that seems like a very hypothetical answer but it’s the reality answer that every decision you make about optimization, needs to be based within the frame of the reality that most people who are attracted to you, into your world now, are not going to buy now.

And the ones who are going to buy are going to buy later. And three emails versus five emails, for a relationship building series, where is the payoff that you’re looking for? Are you trying to get faster to a sale for those 15 to 20% who are going to buy in the next few weeks or month? Or are you willing to sit back a little bit and say, no, we need to make sure that this system really takes advantage of the 80 plus percent who are going to buy later. And I don’t think three emails is enough for that audience.

I think that the stage that we set in the beginning, is the frame that everything will follow in. So take the time now. You can do things. Somebody asked later about a super signature. You can do all kinds of things to get the people who want to buy now to buy. The people who are going to buy will figure out a way to buy. They’re going to do that. Just don’t do it at the expense of everybody else who’s waiting for you to screw up and give them a reason not to buy. Like that’s, I don’t know, that seems like a rant.

I knew I was going to rant today. But that’s the rant, right. Like you can’t argue with gravity. The fact that most people buy later, that’s gravity in our world. We can choose to ignore it but we’re still gonna come crashing down to earth, every single time we jump, whether or not we like gravity. Okay, your turn, say something smarter than I said, that felt like a rant.

[André] I’m gonna frame that and put it on a poster, a fact of life, no different to gravity, Shawn Twing.

[Shawn] There you go.

[Shawn] We’ll write our relationship building series for those later, for sale on the TLB store, later.

[André] Yeah. The only thing I’ll add is, you know, when you’re looking at different versions of reality. So if you look at somebody who builds funnels. Like a funnel, funnel, where there’s no distractions, there’s a single path, an ABC, something happens. When you’re looking at the stats. The only stats you could see, is how far people made it into this thing. How many people have bought, how many people haven’t and where the drop off is. That’s it. That’s the only context that exists in that narrow path, which is not reality.

That’s just someone’s perception of reality. Or forcing something, you know, on a portion of people. But the actual reality is that, all of this is nonlinear. We have people that will come in and make a purchase and then I’ll check in the system because, I’m a curious person and they’re not even on our email list, well they are at the point of purchase but they didn’t exist before that. And then there’s other people that have opted-in and received every single email we’ve sent and they’re all green dots, which means they opened and they clicked something.

But they haven’t made a purchase yet. And everyone’s journey through this thing, through our world, is nonlinear in the sense that it’s not a predictable path. We can try and force some path on people. And some people will wander down that path. But most people won’t. Or they just won’t make it to the end of it. They’ll just wonder around. This is what we do. And it’s impossible to predict what that is, other than creating a world for people to inhabit by doing that and allowing people to interact with it in a way that makes sense to them, it doesn’t make sense to us.

But we just put it there so they can interact with it. And they will then purchase when they’re ready. They’ll do things. There’ll be moments that happen when someone decides, I’m a customer. But there’s no transaction that’s happened. That’ll happen a bit later, on their timeframe. That’s not for us to force upon them. So, you know, I think our world, what we’ve created, more closely represents reality, in the sense that if you go to, you know, tlb.com, there is no linear path.

It’s set up in such a way that people can wander around and in a way that makes sense to them. And when we are paying for traffic and we’re sending it to product pages, they can still wander around. We’re not preventing people from wandering around, you know, we put them on a page and we’ve designed a page in such a way that there is a sense of progression. They can see what’s in front of them but at the end of the day, they can take that path or they can wonder around and go off pieced and they do. So we just think it’s better to, you know, create something that more closely represents gravity. That’s the end of my rant.

[Shawn] This is gonna be my last rant on this subject. Don’t believe your metrics either. When I say don’t, you know, a bunch of you are like, should I just get rid of good landlines? Don’t mean that. Don’t believe the story that your metrics are telling, like that’s the full story. You can only see the parts so far down the line. Yes, if you have a highly optimized funnel.

And there’s only one way into it through a certain type of paid traffic. And then you can see everything that happens, then sure, believe your metrics but also recognize everybody’s coming into it in a different context and people who buy, they’re not buying for the reasons you think. I mean, I just had this experience recently where I just made a purchase from a local accompany near me. It’s a coffee roaster near me. So when they look at their metrics, if they look at their orders, as they come in, they’re going to see that I’m direct traffic.

So I just typed in the URL and then, I’m sorry, they’re going to see that I Google organic traffic. It’s branded. I searched for the name of the company. And they’re going to look and say, oh, he lives right up the road. He probably drove by, probably knows, whatever. They’re gonna craft their story. And their story is completely wrong, right. The actual story is, I got weirdly interested in coffee recently, Gear Patrol has a website or has a top 25 coffee roasters in the United States. I looked at the list and sort of said to myself, all right, if I’m going to try coffee. I’m going to kind of go through this list. The very first entry on the list happens to be a roaster, like an hour from my house. And I live in the middle of nowhere.

So that’s how I was exposed. I didn’t buy it from a McD. Because they have this goofy thing on their site every day of the week there’s a message on their site that says 15% off on Mondays. And I’m sure somebody there, can’t figure out why their sales suck on every day of the week, except Mondays. But it doesn’t say it on Mondays. It says it all the time, it’s crazy. But like, there’s all these things that influenced my purchase and how I found out about. All of them are invisible to them. So the data tells them a story that has no relationship to reality.

And that’s why we keep orienting back to this idea that when you understand some fundamentals of reality, then you can make decisions that you may not be able to go find in your analytics platform. Exactly why that decision made sense. But when you look back on your business from an appropriate timeframe, a year, six, long enough timeframe, it’s all going to make sense. Because the world is just too damn complicated to reduce to a series of, you know, this step leads to this, that the world is not linear. It is systemic. The foundation of the art of email is accepting that reality, that we’re not going to be able to figure out all of these things.

So what we do instead, is we build a system that works as it works within our understanding of reality. And we just have to trust that by making that one decision, everything else that follows is going to be better, as a result. That’s what I should have said earlier, okay. Next question from Mark. This is actually really, this is a great question. Can you say more about how you define absolutely no selling? It looks like your relationship building emails still have links to your courses page and the signature and his current signature to use a Dean Jackson style, super signature that does something similar. For those of you who are not familiar with Dean Jackson’s super signature, is this, it’s something you include in every email that sort of has this idea of like, you know, these are the three ways I can help.

You know, I can help you, I’ve got X XYZ, free courses. I’ve got such and such whatever, you can work with me directly. But it’s a single signature appended to every email that has kind of the overview of the types of audience interaction that you have. Like, you have people who maybe, you have free content for. And then you have people who want DIY content and maybe you have a, done for you service. But you’re breaking it down. That’s sort of the central idea of the Dean Jackson super signature. So, we say no selling but we have links to courses in our signature.

He says, presumably it’s fine to send them to a relevant, valuable article or video, which has an option on the page for interested people to click through to a product sales page. I’m trying to get a feel for how to balance not selling a relationship building series while still having paths that allow fast movers to potentially discover relevant products and buy if they want to. I’ll tackle this André and hand it off to you.

Because I havelike one thought on this. I think, the reason we say absolutely no selling, is because, left to their own devices, most people who are interested in email marketing because of conventional wisdom are going to figure out some way to sell all the time, right. They’re just going to assume that a relationship building series is actually a relationship building, selling series, right. Like people are going to fill that in because either they exist in this, the water that they’re suspended in, is sell, sell, sell, sell, sell. So they’re going to add the context to everything that we include. So it’s gonna be like, oh, you know, like I’ve got a 10 part relationship building series.

And we look at it and it’s two pages of relationship building and eight pages of selling. And he goes like, no, no, I’m building relationships because I relate better to people who buy my shit, right? No! No, stop, right. So I think we’ve taken this sort of hard line position. Of course, there’re going to be fast movers. You know, of course you want people who are coming in saying, I want this thing that you have. We’re not saying no, no, no, hold on. But we’re also trying to save people from themselves. And we’re not trying to be jerks about it. But we know the world is telling you to sell all the time. And in some kinda sneaky ways or the world is telling you to persuade or presuede, you name it. No, don’t do that.

Empathy, authority first, right. It’s empathy, authority, credibility, sell later. When you do that, everything else works. The fast movers, put a super signature in, let them go click through your stuff. I’m a fast mover. You know, I’m not gonna read, you know, the great irony of all of this is, I’m probably not going to read your relationship building series. I’m gonna go buy your stuff. And I’ll be in the customer onboarding series. But I don’t represent the majority of people. I represent people who tend to buy a lot faster, most of the time. Plenty of examples, where that’s not true. So, okay that’s, I’m really just ranting today. I don’t think I’m really answering questions, I’m just ranting. Okay, so, how do you think about the absolutely no selling perspective?

[André] Yeah, I mean, you’ve said it already. I mean, it’s just, don’t be explicit and overt about selling in your RBS. That’s really what we mean. There’s gonna be, as you said, fast movers, implicitly within the dynamic of that RBS, whether it’s a general one or product specific one, it’s gonna create tension and pull people forward and they’re gonna wanna buy anyway. And, you know, you can give them an opportunity to buy, whether it’s in the signature or whatnot but you know, the series itself is not a selling thing.

[Shawn] Yeah and if we open that door a little bit, everyone’s gonna drive a truck through it. That’s part of it. Just think about relationship building series, the way we’ve described it. We even named it this way. The goal of the relationship building series is to establish that relationship and to have, you know, when everybody in your market screams, the person who whispers stands out, right.

When everybody in your market is, you know, doing a two part indoctrination series or three parts and then immediately moving onto the sale. And everybody expects that, you stand out when you don’t do that. That’s just, it’s fact, okay. This is a question from Yvonne. Out of coffee too. So maybe that’ll help with the ranting.

[André] We’re almost half way.

[Shawn] Oh, my word. I can’t believe you just said that. All right, this is from Yvonne. Question number one, do I need a completely different relationship building series for my newsletter subscription? I have an RBS for selling an online course. It’s being revised and include world-building and the beliefs that prospects need to have. No, I don’t know, like, trying to think like, completely different from what.

André, do you have a thought on this one? I’ll go to the next question, maybe this gives us some, I’ll read all the questions. Maybe these are all woven together. They’re just listed separately. So she says, as it is now, when someone does subscribe to my newsletter, they get access to the archive and then gets sent the next newsletter was published. But you write, before any subscriber can receive a newsletter broadcast, they have to go through a relationship building series. This is non-negotiable. So the question is, do I need to start sending them out? Yes, yes, yes, yes. When they sign up for your email, they get the relationship building series first, they do not get the newsletter until after they’ve gone through the entire relationship building series. That’s the answer.

[André] Yeah, when you read the, our lesson, our module on the daily newsletter and we cite many examples, you know, we are not in the newsletter business and some of those examples, even most of them that we cite in the second part of that module, those people have newsletter businesses. Many of them are on sub-stack. You know, when you browze into their page, the very first thing you see is an opt-in box. And then there’s a little link if you wanna see some of the archives. They are in the newsletter business and we’re not.

We just happen to use a newsletter as a mechanism within the system to do what we need it to do. So even if you have a newsletter and you want to emphasize the fact that you have a newsletter on the site, the RBS should come before that, unless you’re in the newsletter business. But even then, I think there should be something that happens before the newsletter. Even if you’re in the newsletter business and you have a paid newsletter.

[Shawn] Yeah, I mean, it helps people understand what to expect, right. It doesn’t have to start in the relationship. It doesn’t even have to start at like, in the emails themselves. I don’t sign up to email newsletters blindly, I mean, first of all, tell me what it’s about. Give me the info. And then when I say, yeah, I’m interested in this, then tell me how to get the most value from it, right.

Tell me about yourself. Why do you do this? Like, what got you here? How do you understand what, you know, my world, right? That’s empathy. Don’t just assume because I signed up for your email newsletter, that that’s really what I care. I’m looking for something. Your newsletter is just a vehicle for me to find this something that I’m looking for. So help me do that and build that relationship. And I’ll thank you for it. You know, with my continued attention for a very long time.

I mean, that’s the idea here. Okay, this is from Matt. There’s a preamble. And then there are five questions. I am going to read the preamble and I think we’ll just tackle the questions one by one. ‘Cause I think they’re separate questions. Okay, here’s the preamble. My business currently utilizes the video sales letter funnel that builds an email list. Which we’ve been email using the opposite approach, the art of email. We do $2.5 million a year in sales with this approach. And have done seven figures plus for the past five years with this model.

I want to shift us off the short-term, first 15% of customers models in the morning, integruous model you’re promoting. I’m trying to figure out how to radically shift into our first relationship building series and build our world from there. Okay. Cool. This is fun and mad respect to you, Matt. That’s a very legitimate thing that you’ve done. Yeah, I can’t remember, was it 85% of businesses fail within the first five year or don’t make it to year five, some ridiculous numbers. So to have a business that still exists after five years, a seven figure business that still exist, is incredible.

You know, it speaks to two things, clearly. One obviously speaks to the effort and hard work that you and your team were putting in but it also speaks to the fact that you can build businesses simply by serving them a minority of an audience who were wanting to buy now. And that’s one of the reasons it’s so attractive, right because you can, there’s nothing that says that you can’t go out in the world and do this, use this business model. Many people do. But what Matt has pointed to as well, is that once you recognize that those really impressive results you’ve been getting are just scratching the surface of what’s possible, then you realize that the $2.5 million a year business that you have is probably a 12 to $15 million a year business. If you change the model. And that’s exciting. Okay. So questions.

In relationship building series video, you discussed using a landing page and it’s almost like the first email in the series. Do you send cold traffic to a page like this to generate your opt-ins? If so, can you put in specific numbers for what you expect leads to cost using this method, if lead cost varies. Okay, so, I’ll jump on this one first and then André you can jump on it as I try to get through this quickly. So, we do that. Our landing page is the beginning.

They’re just sort of some rationale for how we think about this. Rather than ask people, like, we could build curiosity and hide the content and get people to opt-in, which gives us a false signal. Because we’re gonna need a lot of people who are curious, who want to know what that first page of email is, like, it’s really easy to skew your numbers, to get a lower cost per lead but a lower quality of lead at the same time. So to jack up quality of lead and I’ve done this a lot outside when I was working with clients, before André and I teamed up.

I like to put email number one, so it’s visible without an opt-in for people to see, gives you a chance to write a really good email too, to really capture attention. But if someone reads that and they’re not interested and they don’t opt-in, that’s fine with me, right. Because the person who’s doesn’t resonate with that, isn’t going to be a customer, likely. So I’ve just saved myself, you know, reducing my cost per lead, giving me a false signal of quality.

I mean, I’ve cleaned up my metrics a lot, by showing somebody something that they didn’t need to be curious to get to see, right. And you know, when you ask the question, we put specific numbers what you expect leads to cost. I’m not going to go on a rant. I probably am, all right. I’m gonna rant about cost per lead. I think cost related to me is the most ridiculous metric in the world I could a, like my father used to say, I could give a rat’s about cost per lead, right. It’s AOV, right.

It’s average order value. And in your business model, it’s average order value. It’s CPA AOV. That’s the front end business model, period. And then LTV. You know, when you’re generating leads in a 15% business, that’s all that matters. I don’t care what a lead costs. All I care about, is the cost to acquire a customer and how much I’ve made back on that customer in their first sale. And then whatever their LTV is over time. Like that’s all I care about in the business model. I don’t give a shit about cost per lead. There’s so many ways that cost per lead can go wrong because you can start optimizing for leads, take your eye off the ball, AOV, customer acquisition costs and average order value, clean up anything that CPL screws up. Oh, I like that. That sounds kind of fun. All right, André, anything to add on this first one?

[André] No, I don’t think so. Our friend, Jonathan, I mean, his front end is a fairly lengthy long form page, isn’t it? Like 2000 words.

[Shawn] It’s 1600 words, yeah.

[André] 1600 words.

[Shawn] But he’s a perfect example of this. I mean, what did he tell us? Like his, you know, his opt-in rates like 7% but he doesn’t care, right. You know, his customer acquisition cost and so for Jonathan Boyd, that’s the person you mentioned earlier, today is his birthday, coincidentally enough.

Jonathan Boyd has a guitar school, online guitar school. We interviewed him last year, I think. His has success in the course of a year, was mind numbing. And he’s a wonderful human being too. But his business model is paid traffic to a 1600 word landing page. After somebody opts-in there, they’re shown, I believe a VSL. At the time, it was a $27 offer. He doesn’t optimize for CPL, for cost per lead, all he’s looking at is cost to acquire a customer at average order value in that model. I think he said on the call and this was last year, that like is opt-in rate’s only 7%.

But that’s not what he’s optimizing for, you can jack it up, you can do a million things to increase opt-in rate. You can do a million things to reduce cost per lead. But at the end of the day, we’re not in the opt-in rate business. We’re not in the cost per lead business, unless you’re a lead generator, we’re in the customer business, right. So how much does it cost to acquire a customer and how much does that customer spend on their first sale? And then over some period of time that’s meaningful to your business. How much does the customer spend?

You know those three numbers, you understand your business. In this particular business model. In our business model, we don’t sell on the front end. So if you don’t sell on the front end, then you have to have something, first of all, that tracks in an appropriate timeframe. But you also have to be willing to make a certain bet that you’re going to pay for traffic and that traffic is going to monetize with LTV, with long-term value, not with average order value on the first sale. That’s the realtor getting off the reservation here.

We’ll jump on a next question from the same person. What role does an ad have as lead into a relationship building series? How do you conceive of an ad writing within the context of the art of email? Oh, that’s a fun one. Maybe that’s a momentum builder workshop. I think about a, particularly a long copy Facebook ad, as the frame for everything that follows. So, in framing is a really powerful, powerful tool, right.

When you set the frame, you’ve established the boundaries of everything that follows. So if you set a frame that the key to losing weight is diet, not exercise, now you’ve heard the old expression, you know, you lose ounces in the gym and pounds in the kitchen, right. That’s a powerful frame. So, if I’m trying to sell a supplement or anything to do with food going in the mouth, supplements, like, losing weight in general, in my position and eventually the product is going to focus on eating. Then the frame I want to set in an ad, is that this idea of you lose, you know, ounces in the gym, pounds in the kitchen, that killing yourself in gym often can be counterproductive, too, right.

You go to the gym, you kill yourself. You’ve seen a Smash it at your local CrossFit box and you’re hungry as hell by the end, you’re fatter 60 days later, right. That’s a powerful frame for somebody thinking about losing weight. Is trying to choose between diet and exercise and you have a solution that’s diet based. The evidence is on your side. So that’s how I think about using an ad, is framing everything that I’m going to say that follows. I also like to just, side tangent, I like to open and close loops that pull attention through the course of the ad.

I generally don’t like to have a big loop open that requires that they go to the landing page to figure it out because then I get a false signal that they just want the answer. But I do like to have something implicit in the ad and open loop in the ad that is closed by the purchase of the product, eventually. That there’s something only for me to look down the road at and realize that the big thing that’s attractive, whether it’s usually an emotion, the thing that they really want more than anything else, that I’m going to hint at in the ad somehow, is resolved when they eventually become a customer later on. I’m not gonna encourage them to do it tomorrow. But eventually the big payoff. I like to set that up. Any thoughts to add on that? I know you’re a big ad guy in your spare time, you’re sitting around writing Facebook ads.

[André] Yeah, only that I think the big leverage point is in, isn’t coming up with really good, compelling frames and big ideas. And these things that people can be drawn into. It’s not necessarily the underlying system, you know, that’s just there, it’s what you plug into it, you know. Soap opera series is a soap opera series. But there’s a million different versions of that. You can have amazing ones are all ridiculously terrible ones and it’s not the fault of the soap opera series. It’s what’s being put into it.

In the same way that any system that fails, it’s not the fault of the system. It’s the little things that you’re putting into it. So I think, you know, one massive leverage point is coming up, spending time to come up with. And I think Jonathan mentioned in an interview that we did with him, it’s on our website. You know, if you just go to the doable business landing page, I think it’s linked up over there. He mentioned what his one was for the guitar business. I’ve forgotten what it is. And I don’t have it in front of me. Things were kind of like, until that moment.

And then suddenly everything changed. His underlying thing didn’t change. He still had a 1600 word, you know, landing page, that didn’t change. It’s what was on the landing page that changed. And that’s where the leverage comes. It’s unlikely you’re gonna figure that out right out the gate. It’s gonna be a process of a duration. And the more you get to know a market and the people flowing through this thing that you’d created, these opportunities are gonna present themselves, especially when you put something out there and you see that the little needle moves meaningfully and then you can double down on that idea. That’s what I’ll add.

[Shawn] I’ll just mention, that’s process to my American friends out there. You know, something earlier, here’s a perfect example earlier, you know, talking about Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu, right. So here’s the compelling argument for Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu. So I’m doing an ad for a local Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu school. And I’m focusing on adults who, you know,, want to defend themselves or women who want to defend themselves. It’s another popular sub-market. And one of the things, this is a compelling frame is to say, you know and you can come up with some statistic or you can just say most or whatever. But what I hear a lot is, you know, 90% of all fights go to the ground.

So therefore, you want to, you know, if you’re interested in a martial art, accept the reality that most of the time, you’re gonna end up on the ground, rolling around fighting. And the modern martial art gets designed for that environment. Those of us who are stand-up fighters, I’m a Taekwondo black belt, you know, I like to say that, you know, it’s true that 90% of fights go to the ground but that’s because people like me, knock other people do the ground. But that’s not a compelling frame for a Facebook ad. So one other thought on this question on Facebook ads and lead is to relationship building series is, you know, not to get too far into the weeds on Facebook advertising.

There’s no one entry point into your world, from Facebook. It’s a large audience that have different angles that are going to have, that are going to appeal to different segments of that audience. And what you can exhaust. You can have a certain frame that exhausts a tiny part of the audience. And it feels like you’ve exhausted the whole audience but you need lots of different ways, you know, to come at different frames to really maximize what’s going to appeal to a large audience. So don’t get caught up in a single frame like that. Like Karma just let me know that’s not a true statistic that X number of fights go to the ground. Yeah, don’t trust me on anything about that.

I’ve heard that a lot over the years. For those of you in the audience who are Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu practitioners, I have mad respect, I’m just joking around. Okay, question number three. Do you think a simpler squeeze page type landing page without relationship building, seems a pretty good reason to opt-in could work? Headlines, subhead, opt-in, to get access to the relationship building series.

Have you tested this type of ad, a short landing page using the relationship building series as a lead magnet approach versus a longer form landing page subscribe option at the very bottom. You want to tackle that? I know you love this, you made a career doing the short squeeze. What do you think about this short squeeze to the relationship building series?

[André] You know, it’s a decision that somebody needs to make is, do they want to do the heavy lifting before somebody ops-in or after somebody ops-in? And I’ve always wanted to get the highest quality and I’m just, you know, put quality in inverted commas. It’s the certain type of person to come on to an email list thinking a certain thing ,like, there’s a certain frame has been set up and that person, for me, has always been better. So that’s what I’ve always done.

Yeah, I’m sure you can get a one sentence squeeze page with an obvious behind it. And, you know, maybe then your open rate is 30% percent instead of 60%. Well we can’t really look at open rates anymore but you know, just hypothetically speaking, could you make that work? I’m sure you can. But for us, it’s just, we prefer to build a world and interact with people that are on the same page.

And I just think you can do that better when that frame has been set up and it’s purposefully pulling the right people forward and repelling the wrong people. And it’s impossible to do that in a single sentence. You know, I’m just being flippant here, the thin squeeze page being a single sentence but, yeah.

[Shawn] Yeah, in a way I think about this question, is that, this question you’ve heard us ask over and over again from both Liam Ethop and Hunt-Davis, whatever his name is. Will it make the boat go faster? Right. So whatever is, you know, the boat in this example, your business, faster is what? Is faster leads? Right. No, right. Again, you’re not in the lead business, you’re in the customer business.

So every decision should be evaluated by this simple question. Will it make the boat go faster? And faster is, will it acquire more customers? And, maybe. And I’m not even sure that’s faster. In this case, ’cause you could really, you could acquire a ton of customers with, you know, pairing down your offer or whatever. So, you know, well, it’s hard if. This is a hard one to decipher, actually. You know, if, how would we define faster?

Okay, let’s think about it this way. If you believe that you’re getting the minority of the market now, with the methods that you’re choosing and you want to go after the large section of the audience that you think you may have been missing so far to really grow the business, then we need to figure out what those signals are that would tell us that’s happening. And what we would know immediately, is those signals are not to be found in the first 90 days, right.

I mean, that’s, we know that, we’re looking for the people who don’t buy the first 90 days. So anything that we’re doing that drives action in the first 90 days, anything we’re optimizing for but inherently drives action in the first 90 days, means we’re looking in the wrong place, right. What we’re looking for are actions that we take now that have a payoff beyond 90 days. So what you’ve described here, is changing up, making a simpler squeeze page and getting people into relationship building series to opt-in.

What impact does that have on the post 90 day audience? I suspect the answer to that is none. You know, like, as I like to say, you know, round it up to a whole number, the answer is zero. So what I would do to answer, the place that I would be looking into your businesses, is look at all of your unconverted leads that you’ve managed to generate over the last and yes, so look in two places.

All of the unconverted leads that you’ve generated, I would put together a soap opera series to reintroduce you to them and reposition the offer. Knowing that many of them were never going to buy it in the first place. Then you’re reestablishing the relationship and you’re moving them towards, some percentage of them will probably buy. Now, if you do that really well. So if you do relationship building series, I’m sorry, you can do a soap opera series, to a hard bridge, to a story powered promotion.

That’s what I would do with all of your unconverted leads. Then I would change my lead gen, so that it’s not so focused on leads, not so focused on, even customers. I know it’s terrifying to say that. But that it’s much more focused on qualitative versus quantitative signal. Like that’s where I would put my energy and attention. So put off a few more barriers in the beginning. Don’t try to look for ways to remove barriers and lower the threshold into your world.

Look for a few strategic places to put barriers, to get people who are never going to become a customer anyway, from entering your world to begin with. Cloud is your metrics. Okay. Last, nope, fourth question from this same person. What, if anything, do you do in your scenario to handle the people, your relationship building series naturally repels.

I love this question. Do you have a criteria such as contact X didn’t open any of the relationship building emails that then remove someone from your list? Or do you do anything else? So list hygiene. You wrote about list hygiene at one point, didn’t you André? Not too long ago, didn’t you describe the sort of systematic list hygiene?

[André] Yeah. Yeah.

[Shawn] This is scary now though, right? I mean, what’s the metric? Can’t be open rate? Right. We can’t do lists, you know, with the advent of iOS 15. And we don’t have reliable data for open rates. We can’t do list scrubbing now, based on open rates.

[André] Yeah.

[Shawn] Because there are lots of people who open our emails that, it’s not showing up, I guess iOS 15 makes it look like everybody opened. I don’t know, I don’t have a straight answer for that one. I think the straight answer this one is don’t use open rate to decide if somebody is engaged or not. That ship has sailed, thanks iOS 15.

[André] Yeah, exactly. You can still track clicks. So, you know, that’s a metric you can use if you wanted to base your list hygiene on. If people haven’t clicked over a period of time, that can still work. You can put them into a series.

[Shawn] Just make sure you actually give them lots of opportunities to click, right. This is one of those things where things can go horribly wrong. It’s like, well, you know, I’m optimizing for clicks. Well how many links do you put in your emails? Well shit, I never thought of that, right. Like, make sure your whole system is designed for this reality before you start making decisions based on it.

No, I put one link in every three weeks. Wander why nobody’s clicking on my links. I think I’m punch drunk at this point. All right, let’s go on to question number five. Shawn mentions in the relationship building series video, that this is a quote, always terrified to see what I actually said. The system is responding to the audience. Is anything happening beyond allowing people to self-select via clicking the links to be added to various sequences based on their interest. I need to think about this.

[André] We tend to be ’cause you can make things very complicated. And the more complicated and I’ve seen some people’s systems that are very complicated to the point where it’s near enough impossible to make any changes. It’s just so, there’s so many stuff. And I have a friend who, he’s very technical, he’s got a course on ConvertKit and his stuff can be so, so technical. That it’s hard enough just to wrap your head around it, let alone implement it and turn it into a reality. So for us, we tend to have simpler systems.

So, which is expressed inside of the art of email, the most complex thing is the story power promotion, you know, ’cause there’s an automation that controls that. But outside of that, everything is just very simple, yes. And behavior is based on clicks. That is another reality because otherwise, what do you base it on? Just on, you know, engagement is somebody interacting with an email. And part of that is them raising hands at appropriate times.

And the only way to really get that working is for them to click link. So if you ask people to click a link because something’s going to happen after that, the people that are interested will take the link, it’s simple as that really. You know, our CRM or, you know, the tools that we use, they don’t have the level of sophistication that maybe some of these crazy ones do. But that’s a good thing for us, I think.

[Shawn] Yeah, I mean, I’ve worked with clients who have, I mean, I kinda get the intent of the question and it’s an interesting question. If you have an enormous list or if you have a large list within that list are segments that they’ve done things, they’ve responded to certain things, there’s some combination of factors, like maybe they own a certain product that you have but not others, you know, you can layer on all of these things and that’s very sophisticated. We shouldn’t speak to it because, you know, we can talk about it in theory and certainly I’ve seen the value of it, that value I’ve seen, has been really in one of two ways, one, it’s been obvious for somebody like me to figure out, meaning I’ve worked with clients where they’ve done an in-person event and they’ve, you know, it’s like a multi-day in-person event. And they reached out to their audience that was within driving distance.

It was sort of a last minute thing. And they sort of put together a list that way. Like, just some simple stuff like that and you know, people who owned every course, this client had ever created. And that’s just not us, so we shouldn’t speak to it. All right, André, I have a question for you. Just wondering what we should do here. I wonder if we should switch to, it’s 10:30. All right, let’s just finish. I thought for some reason we had, I was looking at, I guess there’s the previous questions in here too, my bad. Just pretend I’m not here. Okay, next question from Joe. We previously would have a general indoctrination sequence that would run a parallel to a lead magnet delivery sequence, indoctrination focused on the brand promise and expectations, lead magnet delivery, focused on getting results, building trust, transitioning to the next step and the art of the email model. Does that mean every lead magnet and product essentially starts with the relationship building series? We don’t click, do they continue through a generic relationship building series?

Oh yeah, there’s just a lot here. And I think the first thought here is, yes, we’re not trying to make a system complicated for the sake of complication. We’re trying to make a system that is valuable to the person who is engaged with it. So if you’re using a lead magnet to generate leads and you have two series running side by side, one that you’re calling an indoctrination series, another to deliver the lead magnet, it seems natural to put those two things together and to simplify and to enhance value, to pull attention forward. So you’re building a relationship within the context, you’re building trust.

You’re giving value. You’re showing them how to, if whenever, I don’t know what the lead magnet is but you’re showing them how to extract value from that. So that’s the lead magnet is almost like a theme that ties the whole thing together. The getting value from the lead magnet can be a theme that binds the whole thing. So the first question is, every lead magnet and product essentially starts with RBS. Every entry point into your world starts with a relationship building series. Again, this gets back to the nature of gravity. It’s worthwhile. It’s a strategic upstream decision to build relationships with our audience before we try to sell them anything.

Everything starts with the relationship building series. It just recognizes reality. So the next question. If so, how do you make sure that it is redundant for people who engage with multiple relationship building sequences? So that becomes more of a question of how would that happen? What do they need? What does somebody need to understand? And again, I don’t know the business, so it makes it harder. What is it that you’re building the relationship about? [André] Yeah, I mean.

[Shawn] This is gonna be a problem.

[André] We’re not in the lead magnet business, we’re in the insight business and the relationship business. So, you know, I’m not saying that, you know, don’t create lead magnets if you want to create lead magnets. And for us, our lead magnets essentially is the stuff that lives on the websites before somebody ops-in and or expressed inside of an RBS or a soap opera series, you know, our lead magnets are in inside our emails and it’s content on the website. It’s never a thing that they opt-in to get, other than it being the email series.

So it could be an email mini course. So we never run into this problem. Whereas we’ve got multiple things that we’re trying to give people, you know, somebody comes in, they raise their hand to get our emerging marketing series. There’s only one of those. Or they’ve come in to find out more information about a product specific entry point. Well, you know, so there’s no repeating,, there’s only one art of email, email sequence.

If we had one, there’s only one emerging marketing series, there’s only one entry point of any entry point. So it’s easy to make that work, again, you know, the context matters here. That’s powering this and I think you just need to pick one and go for it. It’s difficult to mix systems together.

[Shawn] I mean, we could map this to an earlier question and answer this question, right. So let’s assume it’s us, it’s always is about us, right.

[André] Yes.

[Shawn] We’re so concerned about our own thing, right. Let’s assume we have a opt-in to learn more about all four of our core products. And someone comes in and you know, they’re looking for the sake of argument, they’re looking for paid traffic, they find the traffic engine, they’re like, oh, hell yeah, this is it, I want to learn more. And they sign up and then they keep looking around.

They’re like what they’re seeing, like, oh, no, whoa, yo, art of email. This is what I, yeah, this is it. And then they opt-in, like, what happens? Like what happens in a scenario where someone has raised their hand for two things? And there is no, I don’t think there’s a canonical answer here. Like one answer is, you know, use automation but then they’re like, hey, you already raised your hand for paid traffic. You know, easy there killer. You don’t need to do too much at once, right. Like that would me off to no end, right.

And so for me, my, you know, people vote with their behavior principle and knowing how I am, if I raised my hand for two things and you tell me about two things, I’m cool with that. ‘Cause I asked for it, right. So I would be okay with that. I could also, you’re agreeing, I thought you’re gonna disagree with me, dammit. I could also see, you know, some sophisticated automation. That’s like, let’s, you know, hey, you’re, we see that you’re interested in these two things and we’re gonna do this other thing as a result.

But, you know, I think in the spirit of keeping things simple, when someone comes in and so resonates with what you have to offer, that they sign up for, to learn about two or three offers, different offers that you have. Now, if it’s the same series for each one, don’t send them three copies, right. Like, that should be obvious. But if each one is different, in meaningfully different, send them at the same time. And if they’re interested, they’re going to read them. If they overwhelm themselves, they might focus on one of them. That’s why you have multiple bites at the apple. I mean, so yeah, I would give people what they’ve asked for, is a general rule.

[André] Yeah.

[Shawn] Next question.

[André] I would do the same thing.

[Shawn] Yeah, really? I thought you were gonna argue with me on that one. All right, surprises. It’s getting late in the day though, so maybe, it’s late for you, it’s early for me. So maybe that’s why, all right. Next question. We already answered this. Not sure what we said. It doesn’t know where it fits in the overall workshop. [André] Yes.
