Three weeks later, when I get a newsletter, I’m like, oh shit, I meant to buy that thing and I go back and I buy it. So yeah, just let the system run and it’ll do its job.

All right. Question from Jen. Hi Jen. My question is about providing proof in your relationship building series, proof in quotes, which gets me, I’m intrigued now. I have expertise in designing and building systems, for example, I want to help entrepreneurs build self management systems and personal knowledge management systems to help improve their products and their workflow. What kind of proof can you use in your business, if your businesses is new? Oh, this is a really good question. This is a really, this is a question that lots of people face.

Am I correct in thinking that sharing my insights and common misconceptions around productivity and my multi-page pre-sell site and sharing my story of how I overcame my productivity problems in a relationship building series is enough proof without needing testimonials and statistics. Love to hear your thoughts on how much proof is enough when you’re building a relationship with new people who are discovering you for the first time and entering your world. I love this question because it’s so hard, everybody first starting out run into the same thing and there’s good news, bad news.

Good news, bad news is when you do this, whatever it is that you’re doing for 25 years from now, you’re still gonna wake up every day, thinking that you don’t know what you’re doing and the world’s about to find out. At least that’s true for me. Proof is far less important. and it’s a distraction over just being interested, right? Like tell your story. And think about it this way. Your story is going to resonate with people who have the same challenge.

And what you’re doing is you’re saying, this is not how you’re doing it but this is what you’re actually doing. You’re saying to somebody, I know you have this challenge. That’s why you’re here, right. You found your way into my world because you have a problem that you’ve probably looked for solutions elsewhere and haven’t found them, that’s why you’re still looking. And here you are. And good news. I had the same damn problem too.

And I probably looked at some of the same solutions that you did And I found those wanting. And it took me days, weeks, months, years but I finally found the thing that works for me. And in order to understand that and to make sure that it’s a good fit for you, I need to tell you a little bit about my journey and what I found along the way. Now you’ve said that, I don’t need to see any statistics. You’re going to detract from it now. Now you have my attention. ‘Cause you’ve shown me that you and I are very similar, except you’re a lot farther down the journey, trying to solve a problem that I still haven’t found a solution to. So now I’m at the edge of my seat. Like, what did you find out? Like what worked and what didn’t?

You know, what roads did you get out and you realized, oh, this was a terrible mistake. Because I probably went down some of those roads too. It’s like, oh, you tried David Allen’s, “Getting Things Done”. You bought “The Label Maker”. You did all the thing and at the end of the day, you’re like, oh, this sucks. I hate this. And then, you know, you had to start over. Oh yeah, I did that too. Like, that’s what the person is saying in your head. And they’re just on that journey. And no statistic, no testimonial, you don’t need any of that, tell them the story. Because you’re telling them their story. And you’re showing them that there is hope. That’s the best story in the world, right.

Which is, I struggled the same. I had the same problem you did. I found a solution. I made a bunch of mistakes. So you don’t have to. And let me show you what I figured out. And I think proof points actually, they detract from that. I mean, yeah, I would so de emphasize proof because you’re in a soft market, in many ways too, with productivity. And I think about Steven Kotler, you know, he’s the flow guy. He’s written a bunch of books and they have a mid tier program. That’s like $5000 called, Zero to Dangerous. So one of their proof points is, that you’re like 400 or 500% more productive when you’re in flow. And they sort of, they put it in an example, like, you know, what would that mean?

If you spent, you know, all of your working time, it so detracts from their message, right. It just sounds like bullshit, right. First of all, you look at the story, you realize it’s like, you know, self-reported. You can’t possibly be in flow all the time. Like, it doesn’t make any sense. So like for the longest time, that proof point made me not make that leap. And eventually I did and I’m glad I did, the course is incredible. But that quote, unquote proof point just set up a potential barrier for me. Because it doesn’t make any sense.

They tell me about the things that I care about. That arbitrary statistic isn’t. So that’s a very long-winded answer to this question but you don’t need proof. You need a good story. That feels honest to the person and authentic to the person listening to it. And it feels like them and they believe that you found something that is going to work for them too because it worked for you. And you’ve explained how. Like, that’s how I would approach this. André, thoughts. Take it from there.

[André] Yeah, demonstrating value, trumps proof all day long. That’s my view. I’ve demonstrated that to myself over and over again, over the years. One of my earlier moments that I can remember is in 2006, I did a case study as a Christmas present to the people on my email list. And I said to everybody, you know, for Christmas, I’m gonna do a live case study out in the open and I’m gonna pick a random product and I’m gonna promote it. I’m gonna bull the landing pages. I’m gonna buy up AdWords traffic and send it to this thing and I’m gonna try and make this thing work and make money right in front of your eyes. And at no point, did people email me and say, yeah but are you an AdWords expert or things like that.

No, I wasn’t an AdWords expert but they were there ’cause they wanted to see me, maybe they wanted to see me get egg on my face and looking like a dick or whatever it was. But I had people’s attention going through their case study. And I was posting all my results on there. And people love that. And there was no proof other than at the end of it, it was either a failure or a success. And, you know, thankfully it worked out. But yeah, I didn’t need proof. But then funny enough at the end of it, I now had proof that what I was doing generated this meaningful result that I could have referred back to, which I did, later on. So I created proof out of nothing. Even though proof didn’t exist before that, it didn’t need to. It was all about demonstrating value.

[Shawn] I think we should create a theme for stuff like that. That’s the, hold my beer, I want to try something, theme. Right. That’s when you’re just like, I’m gonna go do this thing and show you what I do. And I may fail spectacularly but half the people watching, like I can’t wait to see what I learned from this and the other half, like, I can’t wait to see him fall on his face. Yeah, that’s all right.

All right, we’re coming to the, oh, this is the last, no, we have two questions. Two submitted questions and we’ll jump in the Q and A. For all of you still on here, wow. Impressive. All right, this is from Kara. I hope I’m pronouncing your name correctly. Do you have any comments on how to approach writing as a team member, who was either writing on behalf of the face Loyce, the original world builder behind the brand and based on intimate knowledge of her offering an audience or using existing content that she has previously written. This is exacerbated by the fact that she’s currently on maternity leave, so where she would normally review and approve everything, she currently can’t. Shawn, your comment last week about this kind of impersonation feeling gross, rang true for me because it’s not what I want to do. That send a current structure of the business doesn’t seem to lend to any other approach yet.

The situation creates an unfathomable disconnect for me in terms of authenticity. And I’m not sure how to navigate that, except by using my best skills, writing skills. I was thinking you may have some insight from your experience working with clients, telling other people’s stories. That would be helpful in my position as a what. Yeah, so at the end, this is, please excuse a clumsy question. Don’t apologize for any questions as a broad rule. This is a great question. And it’s not clumsy at all. It’s a hard question. I wanna hand it off to you André, so I can think about it more but that’s not very nice. I’ll answer it, then I’ll hand it off to you because this is, there are two perspectives here. One is what we wish were true and the other is what is true.

So what I wish were true, is that this kind of stuff didn’t happen, right. Because, you know, having been the person who experienced it and seeing behind the curtain, as I said before, it felt really gross, you know, to have had a conversation with someone who I thought I was having a conversation with, who’s now a good friend of mine but then when I met him the first time in person and thought we’d, you know, had had some exchanges and realized, no, we didn’t. That was really gross. It doesn’t sound, I mean that, I’m not sure that’s happening in this situation that you described. So, ghost writing is a thing, you know, is there something wrong with it? Again, that’s not our position to say.

I mean, I don’t, I, in an ideal world, I think it would be great if, when someone’s name was on something, they created it but that’s not always true. So, yeah, I think you have a couple of choices here. One choice and this may not be a choice. But I’ll put it out there as hypothetical choice. One way to approach it is you could, you can look at what the person has written so far and you can essentially be, you can be a parallel writer, who, you’re signing the work and you’re writing it while she’s on maternity leave.

But you’re looking at her previous work and you’re sort of, you’re drawing attention to the things and putting them into a context in that whole context, like you’re quoting her. you’re talking about it but you’re an actor in it. And you’re being very clear about that. I just read a book, you know, recently and it’s outstanding. Highly recommended for everyone. It’s called “The Gap and the Gain”. It’s a concept from Dan Sullivan, the founder of Strategic Coach. But Ben Hardy, I think that’s his name. The book is about an idea that Dan Sullivan came up with but the author writes the book about Dan’s idea. And that’s very clear. It’s not ghost written. That relationship is transparent.

I think that’s certainly, that would be the best solution here. If that solution is not available to you, then I think the next question becomes in a ghost writing environment when you’ve made that choice, that, how do you do that well? And then the question just becomes, what access do you have to understand that person’s voice and what you’re looking less about what she said, is you’re focusing more on the thinking behind what she said. Because then it will make it much easier for you to write in that voice because you understand. Like the thinking is the product. Or the written word, the ideas of the product of thinking. And it’s really easy to run out of those ideas really fast. But when you understand the thinking that generated those ideas, maybe in her writing she explains, like you can, it sort of builds a case where she talks about this, this and based on some experience she’s had or, I’m just making this up but, it’s work.

You need to do the work. So to be a good and I’m not a ghost writer, I’ve never written anything that I didn’t sign. So, you know, I know I’m speaking hypothetically here. But I think to be a good ghost writer, is, gets into this research phase again, to really be able to take the work that you have available to you and deconstruct it. Like, what stories does she tell consistently? Does she tell them consistently?

So you have kind of a narrative to work with and then you can see how the ideas relate to the experience. And they put that together in some kind of dossier so that you understand that person. And, much like a method actor. I think at some point you’ll be able, you’ll be able to think, like she thinks because you’ll understand how she thought about what she wrote. And that’s what you’re trying to get back to. It’s not just, what has she said? What thinking did she share that led her to what she believes to be true. ‘Cause that will inform the broader perspective. I think that may be a clumsy answer to a great question. André, your thoughts on this one.

[André] Yeah. I’ll think out loud and see where this goes. ‘Cause there’s a few things perhaps in the author business, it’s fairly common practice to write under a pen name. So, for various reasons. So, our friends at Sterling and Stone have certain book pen names that isn’t written by single author. So there there’s a pool of authors that can write in a certain voice, under this pen name.

The readers don’t feel cheated because they’re buying a book with a pen name on it. Because the stories that they’re getting are consistent through the lens of, you know, from that pen name person, even though the person itself doesn’t exist in real life, they’re okay with that. And that makes Sterling and Stone far more scalable because if a certain writer is busy writing in other series, they can swap him or her out with another person who can write in that same voice. And then it’s business as usual. They can get their books out and share it with an audience that care about reading these stories. And they’re less concerned, you know, who wrote it, so long as it’s a story by an author about a character. So, you know, using that, you can do versions. I’ve seen been done.

And in fact, I’ve been involved in one of them. So a friend of mine has a really big fitness, like a health and fitness space. And he’s the front guy. Although his team is so big that any emails that he writes, he writes ’cause he’s a good copywriter. So he’ll write his emails. So, at no point is somebody else writing his emails for him. But he created a whole bunch of other fictional characters that represent different pockets of people, different groups of people inside of his audience. And those are written as a fictional character. So that allowed him to swap out staff members that can write on the behalf of that character.

So the voice is always consistent. And again, it’s a fictional character. I’m not, I don’t remember if they’re upfront with the audience that this person’s name is not a real person. I don’t remember, this was many years ago. But I think things like that can happen, you know and it’s probably okay, to various degrees. In fact, really quick story. This week, I was reading an article and there’s this Spanish author, it’s a woman’s name and it’s amazing.

This thriller series. And she won this big award and she had to come to this ceremony to receive 10000 euros as a prize. Well, these three characters had to own up and raise their hand and say, well, it’s not actually a woman, it’s three guys. And so these three guys accepted the award. And it only, in that moment, was it revealed that these three guys are the pen names. I mean, other authors behind this woman’s pen name. But until that point, it was completely unknown and you know, nobody felt robbed and undone but yeah, I think that’s, I’ll stop rambling now.

[Shawn] That’s a hard one. Can you hear me okay André? I put one air pod away and so I don’t know if that compromises the mic at all.

[André] Sounds amazing.

[Shawn] Yeah, that’s that deep baritone voice of mine. All right, I hear ya. All right. Last of the pre-submitted questions. I can’t believe it. I love that Nick has a note here, end of call too. If not already expired. I feel like I might have expired already. All right. So this is from Mary. Hi Mary.

So the question is, when having promotions or additional series and people self-select for them, do you keep on sending the value newsletter? You know, this is a good question. I’m not sure I know the answer to this. André, when we do promotions, do we still send people the value newsletter ? When we’re doing a promotion?
[(André] Yes. We do. You could block it out, I’ve done that in the past. So, if anybody’s in a promotional loop, so any promotion, it removes them from the ability to receive a newsletter. You can totally do that. We don’t have any rules that you should or shouldn’t do that.

We don’t. Because at that point, there’s a level of relationship, you know, that relationship’s been established and we have a schedule that we send newsletters on, you know, every second Friday, for example and people expect that. We like to think everybody expects that. So if they’re in a promotion and it happens to be on a Friday that we send our newsletter, well, then they get the newsletter as well. And I think they’re okay with that. But you don’t have to do that.

[Shawn] I think there’s something nice about that too. Because one of the things, one of the decisions that we make is when, you know, like, the Friday that we send the email about, so that was the Friday that the art of email live workshop promotion was closing. And we send out the newsletter and we made no mention whatsoever of the art of email. ‘Cause it’s, you know, maybe this isn’t true but I like to think that when somebody sees that, they’ve been part of something else for two weeks.

And most people reading the newsletter, haven’t been part of that and it keeps it special, right. It’s kind of like, here’s the thing for everybody but you’ve been getting this thing that not everybody’s been getting it and we’re not crossing over too much. Yeah. And it also, you know, in some context, more context, you know, the email that we sent that Friday, when enrollment closed for the live workshop, was kind of a, it was just a fun, light, best of like, not intense marketing stuff. Like, because we knew, I mean, that’s sort of two reasons.

One, the audience centric reason we do that, a chunk of our audience had been, you know, we’d been asking a lot of them for 10 days. And then we were respecting that but also, we were pretty tired at that point too. And it was like, hey, okay, let’s write a funny email. It’s just kind of laid back because I don’t, you know, neither one of us could find our ass with both hands at that point. So, I can’t believe that’s going to be in a transcript somewhere, oh my God.

[André] Yeah and email our email tomorrow is gonna be amazing. Our newsletter is also gonna be a light one.

[Shawn] Yeah, why don’t you set up expectations? Yeah, that’s a great idea, thanks.

[André] There we go, loops finished.

[Shawn] All right, we got a nice open loop there. All right, we’ve got six questions in the Q and A. I like Amanda’s question because we can both give our answers. All right, first question. I mean just, you get a lot of the indoctrinate, that’s even the language. I think that comes out of the digital marketer. You know, there’s a lot of that language on an indoctrination series, right. Anonymous.

I’m still lost when it comes to insights versus information. Explain, maybe give some examples. We’ve covered this in the first call. I don’t know if that means that you were on the first call and you didn’t hear that answer. So I’m hesitant to repeat myself but I mean, insight is something that you’re trying to create that aha, when someone realizes, like when somebody has a moment where they’re, contributing to that conversation a little bit, right.

So you’ve shared something that you can imagine the person sits back and like, wow, I never thought about it that way. Like an insight is it’s different depending on the sophistication of the person listening to the context, for somebody listening. So, you know, if you realize that, you know, whatever, COVID, whatever else, you’ve put on an extra 15 pounds and you think to yourself, oh, I gotta lose some weight. And your mindset is to lose weight you’d go to the gym. And someone says, hey, by the way, you know, overwhelmingly weights lost in the kitchen, not the gym, right.

It’s what you eat. It’s not how much you’re moving. Well I think Tim Ferriss did this in his “4-Hour Body” book. I just remember some example where he talked about, you know, to burn off, like an Oreo, like the caloric equivalent of an Oreo, was like walking the steps of the Empire State Building, it was some ludicrous example, where you’re like, oh right, okay, exercise isn’t the way to lose weight. Like, that’s an insight. For somebody who doesn’t understand that, that’s an insight, right. It’s not just information. Like this thing that you think.

And maybe there’s something there around insight. Is it something you’ve thought might be true is not true. That could be sort of, part of it. [Shawn] All right, next question. I think we’ve answered this a thousand different ways. Do you have to have multiple relationship building series? But don’t start with the answer and go looking for a question. Start with a question and then find the answer to that question. So, no. I feel like a punch truck. Okay, now we’re onto Amanda’s question. You should answer this because my answer is going to be, I think, of the two, the more controversial. So starting with you. What was your process for finding a writing coach?

[André] I have lots of writing friends who are full-time authors. So I initially spoke to a few of them and interacted at events and saw people. And so my first writing coach, I worked with for a few months and, you know, it just wasn’t a great fit for how she was teaching and in me as a learner. So then I paused that one. And then some period of time afterwards, I found a Alita who’s the person that I use now.

And again, I did a similar process. I’d done some due diligence. Found her, asked a few people that I know, have they worked with her? Do they know her? There were some yeses, there were some no’s and end of the day, you know, I reached out to her and said, hey, do you fancy working together long-term? And we can see how it goes and if I’m a good fit for you and if you’re a good fit for me. And then we just started the relationship that way.

And, you know, she’s a hard ass, which is what I need. So, she terrorizes me every two weeks and we’ve been working together for over two years now. So, I’m just a slow learner. That’s why we’ve been together for so long, I think. Yeah, that’s my process.

[Shawn] All right, I was just being a little glib earlier. I just asked Andréwho he was working with and that’s. You know and I think it’s, I mean, the other interesting thing here is to find out if you’re really coachable, right. You know, André’s worked with Aalita for a couple of years.

I think I lasted, I don’t know, a month and a half, maybe two months. And it just wasn’t for me. And partly that was context and timing and it was just, it was a lot more work at a time when I was, I felt like I was already at the max capacity. So I think coaching in general is a really good idea. I think the thing that I would say from my experience is, find the writing coach that syncs up with what it is that you’re trying to figure out how to do. ‘Cause I think Alita is a, she’s wonderful.

But I would consider her more of a general writing coach, like really back. Like she really started, when I started working, we started with Aristotle, right. So it was, you know, logos and pathos. It was like, okay, this is cool. And it was really, it was really valuable. But at the same time, like right now, I’m, you know, way, way, way deep in the weeds on metaphor. And like, that’s the thing I want to focus on. So for me to find like the help that I’m looking for now is much narrower. So if I were looking for somebody now, I would look, it would be a more, I think and that’s just part of how I think about things. I would’ve focused more. But general writing, yeah, just ask André, that’s easy. You’re gonna get like 5000 emails. This next question, I’m not really, I’ll do a quick answer to this one.

So I have art of email, I’m looking to purchase your additional courses, which should I purchase first? So your influence traffic engine, durable business does a durable business contain the basics and sphere of influence. And the traffic engine only have 130 email subscribers, no money for paid traffic, which course should I go with first? So last thing, no money for paid traffic. Take the traffic engine off your list. It’s all about paid traffic.

Next. The other question does a durable business contain the basics of sphere of influence and the traffic engine. No, it does not. It’s designed to go from zero to a hundred thousand using a framework that builds a flywheel that can then be built on. So, which should you purchase first? I would say in general, squeeze every ounce of value you can get out of the art of email first and then reach out to us by email. And we can sort of point you in the next direction. Don’t look down the road and not see the value that’s right in front of you right now. Do you want to contribute any thoughts on that one André?

[André] Yeah, no that’s, good advice.

[Shawn] Thought you were going to say, buy the traffic engine. All right, last question. Oh my God, we’re doing it. The answer about list hygiene. I believe it wasn’t clear enough. If opens or clicks aren’t good for that now, how do you guys actually prove your lists? So just to clarify what we were saying. With Apple’s recent update iOS 15, tracking open rates is no longer accurate, so the next best thing that we can track are clicks.

However, just because we can track clicks, that doesn’t mean that’s something that we can optimize for, or that we can make decisions about because we have to make sure that the emails that we’re sending, that we’re encouraging that, you know, at some sort of rate, that it gives us a clear signal. So if we say, oh, we’re going to prune our lists based on clicks but we very rarely include links in our email. We’ve sort of screwed ourselves right out of the gate. So the decision to prune based on clicks and engagement and defining engagement by clicks, means that we need to structure our emails. And there are lots of opportunities to click to get a clear signal.

But it’s still not an entirely clear signal. Some people are wanting to read the emails beginning to end. And you can write a good email where the links are, you know, rabbit holes people can go down but if this was up to me this is a big challenge right now. Because people can engage with our content and our emails top to bottom, soak it all in, transform their lives from the value that we contribute to them. And there’s not any guarantee that they will have done something to indicate that that has happened anymore. Right. So, I don’t know. I’m actually, I’m sort of curious about how do we actually effectively prune our list going forward?

And it’s, you know, it’s not like the whole world just changed because iOS 15 just was released. But, you know, that’s what’s coming. So we have to be thinking about this. I mean, it’s a hell of a challenge. We haven’t really talked about this launch, André. How are you thinking about this?

[André] Yeah, I mean, our process hasn’t changed that much because of how we do this. So inside of ConvertKit, example, they have a mechanism where you can rank based on some criteria, which they call stars. So, one star, two star, three star, four star, five star. Five star is opening it. clicking everything. So I have a segment created for each one of them. So at a glance, like I can look now and see we’ve got 20 subscribers listed in the one star category, which is the worst category. So I can just click into that and then just interact with those 20 subscribers, you know, just click around and just try and make sense of why they’re there.

And if they really aren’t doing anything. And then what I can do is, I can then launch, I can trigger our engagement series. And I can trigger that at any time. So I can then go into, I can open those 20 subscribers and say, apply re-engagement tag. And then that’ll put those 20 people into re-engagement sequence. And all that sequence would do, I think ours is three or four emails long. And it’s just asking them, so it’s given them four opportunities.

It’s, yeah, I think it’s four emails long. So it gives people four attempts to read it and raise their hand. And they raise their hand by clicking yes, I want to stay. And that click just keeps them on the list, then they’re back in. And you know, Apple can’t block that. That’s, you know, so although the stars, some of the criteria for them to be starred as a one-star, the reason why they’re inside there could be a tracking thing.

But it’s unlikely because ConvertKit will also base their tracking on people clicking links. So those 20 one starred people, they could have read all of our emails, they just aren’t clicking on the links for whatever reason. When they’re suddenly exposed to our four email, hey, do you wanna stay email? Well, most of them are gonna click and say, yeah, yeah, I’m good, I want to stay. Or if they do nothing and completely ignore those four attempts to re-engage with them, then we delete them, I’m afraid. Yeah.

[Shawn] Occasionally piss off somebody who has been reading every email. Like, if you really want it, you’ll need to get an audience that’s engaged for it. Send an email, something like, hey, we see you haven’t been engaging with our stuff. Would you like to stay? And they’re like, hey, jackasses, I’ve been reading every email. Now I want to punch you in the face.

[André] Yeah, I mean, we are actually really careful in that first email. We do draw attention to the fact that, you know, tracking is not accurate and you may be reading all the emails. But the reason why you’re getting this is because you haven’t clicked a link in the last 90 days. And we have lots of links. So that’s a legitimate reason why they’re receiving that email and the next one and the next one. So all they need to do is click and then they’re back on again. And they’re removed from the re-engagement sequence. And then it’s business as usual.

[Shawn] Cool. All right, so that’s all the questions. It’s time to wrap up. My wrap-up is actually going to be pointing to something I mentioned earlier today. Then I’ll hand it over to you André, for the final thing here. So the book I mentioned earlier, somebody posted a link to it. It’s called, A Gap in the Game. This is a really important concept for your journey through the art of email. So here’s the basic idea from Dan Sullivan. In general, what we tend to do is, we tend to evaluate our progress relative to the gap between where we are in an ideal future, right.

So all have you bought the art of email. All of you came up with some arbitrary definition of success in your mind. I’m going to do X, Y, Z and then I will have been successful. And then what happens is you do a bunch of work and because you know the horizon, you never make it to the horizon. It’s always in the distance, you do a bunch of work. And then you look out to what your vision of the ideal was.

And you realize there’s a gap between where you are and that vision of what you hoped you would do. And then you feel bad, right. That’s the gap. The game mentality is when you look out into the future and say, this is where I hope to go, but that what you evaluate for decision-making purposes. And you know, whether or not you were successful is the gap. It’s really looking backwards versus forward.

So it’s where are you now, relative to where you were when you started? How much have you gained, right? Or you can think about it. What’s the gap between, you know, where you were and where you are. However you want to think about it. Whatever’s easiest for you. So when you, to make progress with the are of email, just to be clear, you’re never going to be done. We’re not. We do this all the time, this is what we do. We’re never going to finish.

There are always things to add. There are always sequences to create, series to create and ideas to like, it’s never going to end. And if we imagine some point in the future, when we’re finished and it’s perfect and you know, dollars rain from the heavens and the crowd holds us up as God-like geniuses and we never get there. And we always think, dammit, we’re not there yet. And we’re gonna feel miserable, right. But, if every single time we look wherever we are, we looked back to, you know, whatever, six weeks ago, two months ago, whatever it is and we look back and see the distance we’ve come and how much we’ve accomplished since then. That’s a more accurate indicator.

That’s the game that we play. So I’ll leave you with that today. Hey, we have one minute. We’re gonna tie with last week. So we’re gonna beat it André, don’t mess it up. But evaluate, first of all, recognize that the art of email is a marathon and it is not a sprint. And that you’re going to run this marathon forever, as long as you’re in business. And you’re using email marketing, you’re going to be running this race.

So when you evaluate your progress, evaluate it looking backwards from where you just came from in some period of time, last week, last month, six months ago, last quarter or whatever it is, don’t try to figure out why you haven’t reached that horizon that you’re never going to get to. And it’s just, it’s demotivating. It will make you feel miserable. Okay. Were tied with last times time. So now André, take it home over the finish line, beat the record.

[André] On that bombshell. This is, I think we are getting close to the point in the workshop where we’re gonna be looking at people’s work, which means, you know, you guys need to do the work. So, yeah, I mean, we look forward to reading these email sequences and maybe even, yeah, I mean, just reading all of them and picking one to pick apart and yeah, it’s gonna be exciting. So I think hat ties in nicely with what you’ve just said because, you know, writing your first relationship building series moves you ahead of where you were before.

[Shawn] And if you can’t do a whole series, write an email. If you can’t do it, write an email, write an intro to an email. If you can’t write an intro to an email, send us a sentence, Right. Here’s my lead implication single sentence. But do something. Commit to doing something and send it to us.

We’re gonna look at every single person who submits something. We’re gonna look at it and we’ll choose some that we think are most broadly useful for everybody. So if you’re willing to do the work, we’re willing to meet you there and to look through it. And if you have created something that we see, we think is going to be valuable for everyone, then you’re going to see the two of us chewing through it and give you something. We’ll give you at least our thoughts on how to make it better. So get after it. Hey, we did it, one minute. Excellent. Thanks to all of you who made it all the way. See, ya.

[André] Take care.
